name: Cape Kri
description: World-record fish-density dive — a single survey here counted 374 species in a single dive. Drift along the wall with schooling jacks, snappers, sweetlips, and a constant traffic of reef sharks. Currents shift fast; the dive briefing matters.
getThere: Sorong (SOQ / Domine Eduard Osok) on the West Papuan tip is reached via domestic Garuda, Batik, or Sriwijaya flights from Jakarta (CGK) or Bali (DPS). Most flights are red-eyes that arrive early morning in time for liveaboard departures.

siteSpecificGear.name: Reef hook
siteSpecificGear.reason: Current rips around the cape — hooking in is the only way to watch the schooling action.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB + reel
siteSpecificGear.reason: Drift exits over deep water. SMB is required.

notes: Down-currents reported by experienced divers. Stay with the guide; do not chase fish into the blue.

name: Blue Magic
description: Submerged seamount in Dampier Strait that pulls in oceanic mantas, mobula rays, and big-fish action. Top of the pinnacle sits at 7 m; the sides drop into blue water. Current is the price of admission.
getThere: Sorong (SOQ / Domine Eduard Osok) on the West Papuan tip is reached via domestic Garuda, Batik, or Sriwijaya flights from Jakarta (CGK) or Bali (DPS). Most flights are red-eyes that arrive early morning in time for liveaboard departures.

siteSpecificGear.name: Reef hook
siteSpecificGear.reason: Top of the seamount is exposed to ripping current.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB + reel
siteSpecificGear.reason: Drift surfacings over open water.

name: Manta Sandy
description: Sand-bottom cleaning station at 15 m. Reef mantas line up at coral bommies while cleaner wrasse work them over. Easy dive — limited current, defined manta queue line you stay behind.
getThere: Sorong (SOQ / Domine Eduard Osok) on the West Papuan tip is reached via domestic Garuda, Batik, or Sriwijaya flights from Jakarta (CGK) or Bali (DPS). Most flights are red-eyes that arrive early morning in time for liveaboard departures.

siteSpecificGear.name: Patience
siteSpecificGear.reason: Stay behind the marked queue line. Mantas leave if pushed.

name: German Channel
description: Cut-through channel dredged in 1908 connecting inner lagoon to outer reef. Mantas come in to be cleaned on the sandy bottom, and grey reef sharks patrol the channel mouth on falling tide.
getThere: Koror (ROR / Roman Tmetuchl International) is Palau's only international airport, with direct flights from Manila, Taipei, Seoul, Guam, and Tokyo.

siteSpecificGear.name: Surface marker buoy
siteSpecificGear.reason: Drift exits in the channel — operators require SMB.

name: Blue Holes
description: Four vertical shafts in the reef table that open into a single cavern. Drop through the holes at 10 m, exit at 30+ m through the cavern window, then drift to Blue Corner. Beam-shafts of light through the openings make it one of the most photographed dives in the Pacific.
getThere: Koror (ROR / Roman Tmetuchl International) is Palau's only international airport, with direct flights from Manila, Taipei, Seoul, Guam, and Tokyo.

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive light
siteSpecificGear.reason: Cavern is dark below 20 m — even with surface light overhead.

notes: Most operators run Blue Holes into a Blue Corner drift — single tank, two iconic sites.

name: Bajo Alcyone
description: Submerged seamount 25 m below the surface, rising from the deep. Hammerhead schools in the hundreds patrol the up-current edge. Cold thermocline, surge, and current — the most famous dive in the Eastern Pacific.
getThere: San José (SJO) is Costa Rica's main international airport, with direct flights from across North America, Europe, and Latin America. From there, liveaboard departure from Puntarenas (~36 h crossing).

siteSpecificGear.name: Temperate-grade wetsuit
siteSpecificGear.reason: Thermocline drops to 19°C at depth even in season.

siteSpecificGear.name: Reef hook
siteSpecificGear.reason: Standard procedure: hook in at the up-current edge of the seamount.

siteSpecificGear.name: Nitrox certification
siteSpecificGear.reason: Most operators run nitrox for the long deep profiles required at 25–40 m.

notes: Permit-controlled park. Liveaboard-only access.

name: Barracuda Point
description: Drift along the wall and a tornado of chevron barracuda forms overhead. Add turtles, schooling jacks, and the occasional whitetip shark. Often run as a single drift dive — current dictates direction.
getThere: Tawau (TWU) in southeast Sabah has direct domestic flights from Kuala Lumpur and Kota Kinabalu. From Tawau it's about a 90-minute road transfer to Semporna, where boats depart for Sipadan and Mabul.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB + reel
siteSpecificGear.reason: Drift exits over deep water are standard here.

notes: Permit-controlled — 176 daily permits for the entire island. Book the permit months ahead.

name: Shark Bay (Wolf Island)
description: Northern point of Wolf Island where hammerhead schools sweep through in the hundreds. Hook into the rocks at 18 m, watch the sharks pass at arm's length. Whale sharks in season; dolphin pods occasionally crash the dive.
getThere: There are no international flights to the Galápagos — connect through Quito (UIO) or Guayaquil (GYE) on mainland Ecuador, then take the ~2 hour LATAM or Avianca flight to Baltra (GPS) or San Cristóbal (SCY).

siteSpecificGear.name: Coldwater wetsuit + hood
siteSpecificGear.reason: Humboldt upwellings drop temp to 19°C in season — third dive of the day is the cold one.

siteSpecificGear.name: Reef hook
siteSpecificGear.reason: Hooking is the only way to hold the line while hammerheads pass.

siteSpecificGear.name: Nautilus Lifeline or PLB
siteSpecificGear.reason: Surface separations have happened here. PLB is standard kit.

name: Shark Airport (North Atoll)
description: Reef plateau on the North Atoll where whitetip and grey reef sharks rest on the sand. Drift the wall and stop on top to watch the sharks come and go. Visibility consistently >30 m in season.
getThere: Puerto Princesa (PPS) on Palawan has direct domestic flights from Manila and Cebu, plus a handful of regional international flights. Tubbataha liveaboards depart from PPS during the March–June season.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB + reel
siteSpecificGear.reason: Drift exits over deep water — required by all operators.

notes: Park is open mid-March to mid-June only — strict seasonal window.

name: Big Brother
description: Offshore pinnacle in the southern Red Sea with two wrecks on the plateau — the Numidia and the Aida. Walls drop into the blue where oceanic whitetips, threshers, and hammerheads cruise. Strong current makes it advanced-only.
getThere: Hurghada (HRG) and Marsa Alam (RMF) both have direct flights from across Europe and several Middle East hubs; pick whichever is closer to your departure port. Cairo (CAI) connections work for travelers from further afield.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB + long reel
siteSpecificGear.reason: Drift exits in open ocean — operators want long-line SMB.

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive light
siteSpecificGear.reason: Two wrecks on the plateau, both worth penetrating with a guide.

notes: Liveaboard-only access — no day-boat trips reach Brothers.

name: Fish Head (Mushimasmingili Thila)
description: Pinnacle in North Ari Atoll famous for grey reef sharks circling the top in current. The reef bristles with sea fans and overhangs from 8 m to 30 m. Drift on, hook in at the top of the thila, watch the shark traffic.
getThere: Malé (MLE / Velana International) is the Maldives' only international airport, with direct flights from across Asia, the Middle East, and Europe. From there, seaplane transfer to North Ari.

siteSpecificGear.name: Reef hook
siteSpecificGear.reason: Currents at the top of the thila are strong enough that hooking in is the only way to hold position.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB + reel
siteSpecificGear.reason: Drift exits over deep water — surface marker is required by all operators here.

notes: Best dived on incoming tide. Grey reef shark numbers peak in afternoons.

name: Maaya Thila
description: Iconic night-dive pinnacle in North Ari. By day it's a moderate reef circuit; by night the thila comes alive with hunting white-tip reef sharks, octopuses, and free-swimming morays.
getThere: Malé (MLE / Velana International) is the Maldives' only international airport, with direct flights from across Asia, the Middle East, and Europe.

siteSpecificGear.name: Primary dive light
siteSpecificGear.reason: The marquee dive here is at night — bring a focused beam, not just a backup.

notes: Night dive is the highlight. Day-dive only is missing the point.

name: Manta Alley
description: Cleaning and feeding station on the south side of Komodo where mantas come in to be cleaned by wrasse. Calmer than Batu Bolong, but a long boat ride from Labuan Bajo — usually a liveaboard stop.
getThere: Labuan Bajo (LBJ / Komodo Airport) on Flores has multiple daily domestic flights from Bali (DPS) and Jakarta (CGK). The harbor and dive operators are 5–10 minutes from the airport.

siteSpecificGear.name: Camera (wide-angle)
siteSpecificGear.reason: Mantas can be close enough to fill the frame. Strobes optional in clear water.

notes: Liveaboard south-circuit stop. Day boats from Labuan Bajo rarely make it this far.

name: Castle Rock
description: Submerged pinnacle in Komodo's north — top of the rock sits at 4 m, drops to 40+ m. Strong current splits around the rock and concentrates schooling jacks, trevallies, and reef sharks on the up-current side.
getThere: Labuan Bajo (LBJ / Komodo Airport) on Flores has multiple daily domestic flights from Bali (DPS) and Jakarta (CGK). The harbor and dive operators are 5–10 minutes from the airport.

siteSpecificGear.name: Reef hook
siteSpecificGear.reason: Top of the rock is exposed to ripping current — hooking in is the only way to watch the action.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB + reel
siteSpecificGear.reason: Drift surfacings put you over 40m of water — SMB required.

notes: Negative entry required. Don't surface up-current of the rock.

name: Manta Point (Madivaru)
description: Cleaning station on the eastern edge of South Ari Atoll where reef mantas queue at coral bommies in the afternoon. Outside the cleaning windows the site is a relaxed reef dive at 10-14 m; during peak activity it can feel like manta traffic control.
getThere: Malé (MLE / Velana International) is the Maldives' only international airport, with direct flights from across Asia, the Middle East, and Europe. From there, seaplane transfer (~25 min) to South Ari resorts.

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive computer
siteSpecificGear.reason: Operators here expect divers to track their own NDL and surface intervals across multi-dive days.

siteSpecificGear.name: Surface marker buoy
siteSpecificGear.reason: Boats stack up during peak season; signalling on ascent prevents prop incidents.

siteSpecificGear.name: Wide-angle camera or GoPro
siteSpecificGear.reason: Mantas pass close enough that a standard phone case rarely captures the scale.

notes: Cleaning activity peaks at outgoing tide. Operators usually time the dive accordingly.

name: Batu Bolong
description: A single rocky pinnacle in the middle of Komodo's central straits. Currents split around it, leaving one side calm enough to drift the wall while the other rips overhead at three knots. Nutrient flow keeps the reef carpeted in soft coral and stacked with reef fish from 5 m to 40 m+.
getThere: Labuan Bajo (LBJ / Komodo Airport) on Flores has multiple daily domestic flights from Bali (DPS) and Jakarta (CGK). The harbor and dive operators are 5–10 minutes from the airport. From there, day boat from Labuan Bajo harbour (~2h to site).

siteSpecificGear.name: Reef hook with lanyard
siteSpecificGear.reason: Current can pin you to the wall — hooking onto dead reef saves air and posture.

siteSpecificGear.name: Heavier wetsuit (mid-year)
siteSpecificGear.reason: Southern Komodo upwellings drop the water temperature sharply in June–September. Layer up.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB + reel
siteSpecificGear.reason: Drift exits put you in shipping channels. SMB up before ascent is non-negotiable.

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive computer
siteSpecificGear.reason: Deep, current-rich dives — accurate NDL tracking matters.

notes: Always brief the current direction with the guide. Wrong side of the pinnacle = blown off the wall.

name: Darwin's Arch (The Pillars)
description: Liveaboard-only seamount off the remote Darwin Island. Schooling scalloped hammerheads in the hundreds, whale sharks in season, silky sharks circling the surface. Strong current, surge, cold thermoclines, and the second-best shark-density site on the planet. The arch itself collapsed in 2021; the pillars remain.
getThere: There are no international flights to the Galápagos — connect through Quito (UIO) or Guayaquil (GYE) on mainland Ecuador, then take the ~2 hour LATAM or Avianca flight to Baltra (GPS) or San Cristóbal (SCY). From there, liveaboard departure from San Cristobal (SCY) - Darwin is 30+ hours by sea.

siteSpecificGear.name: Coldwater wetsuit + hooded vest
siteSpecificGear.reason: Cold Humboldt upwellings — hypothermia risk on the third dive of the day if you're under-suited.

siteSpecificGear.name: Reef hook with safety lanyard
siteSpecificGear.reason: Standard procedure: hook into rock, watch hammerheads parade past at safe distance.

siteSpecificGear.name: Nautilus Lifeline or PLB
siteSpecificGear.reason: Surface currents have separated divers from boats here. Personal beacon is essential.

siteSpecificGear.name: Air-integrated dive computer
siteSpecificGear.reason: Multi-tank cold deep dives — accurate gas/NDL tracking matters more than at a warm reef.

notes: Park-permitted operators only. Required certs: AOW + Nitrox + 50 logged dives at minimum (most boats want more).

name: USS Liberty Wreck
description: A WWII US Army cargo ship torpedoed in 1942 and pushed into the sea by the 1963 Mount Agung eruption. The 120 m hull now rests parallel to shore between 5 and 30 m, encrusted in soft coral and sponges. Beach entry makes it one of the most accessible major wrecks in the world.
getThere: Denpasar (DPS / Ngurah Rai International) is Bali's only airport, with direct flights from across Asia, Australia, and a handful of long-haul carriers. Tulamben is a 2.5–3 hour drive northeast along the coast road.

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive light
siteSpecificGear.reason: Penetrable sections of the hull are dark even at midday.

notes: Dive at dawn to beat the day-trip crowd from south Bali.

name: The Drop-Off
description: A near-vertical wall plunging from 3 m to beyond 60 m at the eastern end of Tulamben Bay. Black volcanic sand at the base contrasts with dense gorgonians and barrel sponges on the wall face. Reliable for big fish drifting past in clean water.
getThere: Denpasar (DPS / Ngurah Rai International) is Bali's only airport, with direct flights from across Asia, Australia, and a handful of long-haul carriers. Tulamben is a 2.5–3 hour drive northeast along the coast road.

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive computer
siteSpecificGear.reason: Easy to overshoot depth on the wall — watch your profile.

name: Coral Garden
description: Shallow sloping reef west of the Liberty wreck, seeded with artificial reef structures and bommies that host an unusual density of cleaner shrimp, frogfish, and juvenile fish. Ideal as a checkout or night dive with calm conditions and 5 to 15 m depth range.
getThere: Denpasar (DPS / Ngurah Rai International) is Bali's only airport, with direct flights from across Asia, Australia, and a handful of long-haul carriers. Tulamben is a 2.5–3 hour drive northeast along the coast road.

siteSpecificGear.name: Macro light
siteSpecificGear.reason: Best on night dives when mandarinfish display.

name: Lekuan Walls
description: Three contiguous wall sections (Lekuan I, II, III) along the south side of Bunaken island, dropping from the reef crest to over 200 m. Bristling with sea fans, sponges and overhangs hiding turtles. The cleanest visibility in the Bunaken marine park.
getThere: Manado (MDC / Sam Ratulangi) in North Sulawesi has direct flights from Singapore, Jakarta, and several Asian hubs. Bunaken Marine Park is 30–60 minutes from the airport by car and boat.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB
siteSpecificGear.reason: Boat pickup from drift end of wall.

name: Bunaken Timur
description: The eastern wall of Bunaken island, generally lighter current than Lekuan and consistently good for pelagic encounters in the blue. Big schools of jacks and the occasional eagle ray cruise the edge. Soft coral coverage is excellent.
getThere: Manado (MDC / Sam Ratulangi) in North Sulawesi has direct flights from Singapore, Jakarta, and several Asian hubs. Bunaken Marine Park is 30–60 minutes from the airport by car and boat.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB
siteSpecificGear.reason: Drift exit standard.

name: Apo Reef Shark Ridge
description: Apo Reef is the second-largest contiguous coral reef system in the world. Shark Ridge is a sloping wall along the western edge where whitetip and grey reef sharks rest on sand ledges and schooling jacks form tornadoes overhead.
getThere: Manila's Ninoy Aquino International (MNL) is the Philippines' main gateway, with direct flights from much of the world. Domestic onward flights via Cebu Pacific or Philippine Airlines connect to most dive regions.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB
siteSpecificGear.reason: Drift dive on the wall.

siteSpecificGear.name: Reef hook
siteSpecificGear.reason: Useful when current picks up at the ridge.

notes: Marine park fees apply; remote location requires liveaboard or multi-day boat charter.

name: Binangaan Drop-Off
description: Sheer wall on the northern edge of Apo Reef, dropping into deep blue water. Hammerheads and pelagic thresher are occasionally reported in the blue, and the wall itself is loaded with sea fans and barrel sponges.
getThere: Manila's Ninoy Aquino International (MNL) is the Philippines' main gateway, with direct flights from much of the world. Domestic onward flights via Cebu Pacific or Philippine Airlines connect to most dive regions.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB
siteSpecificGear.reason: Standard drift exit.

name: Pescador Island
description: Small offshore islet ringed by walls and the famous Cathedral cavern at 18 m. Soft coral and gorgonians cover the walls and pelagic thresher sharks are seen in the deeper blue at dawn. Easy boat hop from Moalboal town.
getThere: Cebu's Mactan-Cebu International (CEB) has direct flights from much of East Asia plus a few Middle East and US hubs. From Cebu City it's a 3–4 hour drive across the island to Moalboal.

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive light
siteSpecificGear.reason: For the Cathedral cavern interior.

name: Panagsama Sardine Run
description: A resident year-round bait ball of millions of sardines off Panagsama Beach. The school swirls along the wall in 5 to 15 m, easily accessible from shore. Thresher sharks and jacks occasionally drive the ball into formations.
getThere: Cebu's Mactan-Cebu International (CEB) has direct flights from much of East Asia plus a few Middle East and US hubs. From Cebu City it's a 3–4 hour drive across the island to Moalboal.

siteSpecificGear.name: Wide-angle camera setup
siteSpecificGear.reason: Sardine ball at 10 m demands a wide angle to capture scale.

name: Elephant Head Rock
description: Massive granite boulders forming swim-throughs, canyons and arches between islands 7 and 8. Topography is the draw rather than corals — sun beams cut through the gaps and big schools of fusiliers stream by.
getThere: Phuket International (HKT) is the main southern Thailand gateway, with direct flights from across Asia, Europe, and the Middle East. Khao Lak — the standard launching point for Similan liveaboards — is about a 90-minute drive north.

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive light
siteSpecificGear.reason: Granite swim-throughs are dark in the middle sections.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB
siteSpecificGear.reason: Currents can sweep you off the formation; surface drift exit common.

notes: Marine park closed mid-May to mid-October during southwest monsoon.

name: East of Eden
description: Sloping reef on the east side of Similan island 7 with the densest soft coral coverage in the chain. The famous pinnacle at 25 m is wrapped in pink and purple soft corals and schooling glassfish. Generally protected from strongest currents.
getThere: Phuket International (HKT) is the main southern Thailand gateway, with direct flights from across Asia, Europe, and the Middle East. Khao Lak — the standard launching point for Similan liveaboards — is about a 90-minute drive north.

siteSpecificGear.name: Macro lens
siteSpecificGear.reason: Best dive in the park for small soft-coral critters.

name: Chumphon Pinnacle
description: Granite pinnacle complex topping out at 14 m and dropping to 36 m, located about 13 km off Koh Tao's northwest coast. Massive schools of barracuda and big-eye trevally circle the towers; bull sharks have been resident here in recent years.
getThere: Koh Samui (USM) has direct domestic and regional flights from Bangkok (BKK), Singapore, Hong Kong, and Kuala Lumpur. From Samui take the high-speed catamaran or Lomprayah ferry (~1.5–2 hours) to Koh Tao.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB
siteSpecificGear.reason: Open-water pinnacle — surface drift exits.

name: Sail Rock
description: Solo limestone pinnacle midway between Koh Tao and Koh Phangan, rising from 40 m to surface. Famous for its vertical chimney swim-through from 18 m to 6 m. Reliable whale shark encounters in season.
getThere: Koh Samui (USM) has direct domestic and regional flights from Bangkok (BKK), Singapore, Hong Kong, and Kuala Lumpur. From Samui take the high-speed catamaran or Lomprayah ferry (~1.5–2 hours) to Koh Tao.

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive computer
siteSpecificGear.reason: Profile through the chimney swim-through.

name: Richelieu Rock
description: Horseshoe-shaped pinnacle in open Andaman water, considered Thailand's best single dive. The whole structure is sheeted in purple and pink soft coral and anemones; whale sharks are seasonal regulars. Top of pinnacle barely breaks the surface at low tide.
getThere: Phuket International (HKT) is the main southern Thailand gateway, with direct flights from across Asia, Europe, and the Middle East. Khao Lak — the standard launching point for Similan liveaboards — is about a 90-minute drive north.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB
siteSpecificGear.reason: Open-ocean pinnacle, all dives end with drift surface.

siteSpecificGear.name: Reef hook
siteSpecificGear.reason: Hook in on up-current side to watch the action without finning.

notes: Liveaboard-only access; closed mid-May to mid-October.

name: Paradise Reef
description: Shallow muck and reef site off Mabul that is one of the most reliable critter dives in Southeast Asia. Sandy bottom with scattered coral patches hosts flamboyant cuttlefish, mimic octopus, and a parade of rare nudibranchs. Easy, no-current macro paradise.
getThere: Tawau (TWU) in southeast Sabah has direct domestic flights from Kuala Lumpur and Kota Kinabalu. From Tawau it's about a 90-minute road transfer to Semporna, where boats depart for Sipadan and Mabul.

siteSpecificGear.name: Macro light
siteSpecificGear.reason: Cryptic critters need a focused beam to spot.

name: Mabul House Reef
description: Shore-accessible sandy slope directly off the resort jetties on Mabul. Artificial reef pylons and sea-grass beds harbour seahorses, frogfish, blue-ringed octopus, and gobies on shrimp. The classic unlimited-dive house reef.
getThere: Tawau (TWU) in southeast Sabah has direct domestic flights from Kuala Lumpur and Kota Kinabalu. From Tawau it's about a 90-minute road transfer to Semporna, where boats depart for Sipadan and Mabul.

siteSpecificGear.name: Macro light
siteSpecificGear.reason: Night dives reveal a different cast of critters.

name: Shark's Cave
description: Vertical wall on the southwest corner of the Layang Layang atoll with a recess at 30 m where whitetip reef sharks rest in stacks. Beyond the wall, hammerhead schools cruise the blue in April and May.
getThere: Kota Kinabalu (BKI) on Sabah's west coast is the main entry point, with direct flights from Kuala Lumpur, Singapore, Hong Kong, and a handful of regional hubs.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB
siteSpecificGear.reason: Wall drift exits over open ocean.

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive computer
siteSpecificGear.reason: Watching hammerheads tempts you deep; track your profile.

notes: Resort only open March to August; closed in monsoon season.

name: The Wreck Point
description: Atoll corner where two walls meet, named for a small Filipino fishing wreck on the slope at 28 m. Strong corner currents bring in dogtooth tuna, schooling barracuda and the chance of pelagic action.
getThere: Kota Kinabalu (BKI) on Sabah's west coast is the main entry point, with direct flights from Kuala Lumpur, Singapore, Hong Kong, and a handful of regional hubs.

siteSpecificGear.name: Reef hook
siteSpecificGear.reason: Corner currents will sweep you off the wall.

name: Bradford Shoals
description: Offshore seamount rising from 600 m to a coral-capped summit at 10 m. Schooling barracuda, scalloped hammerheads in season, and an exceptional sea fan and soft coral garden across the plateau. One of Kimbe's premier blue-water sites.
getThere: Hoskins (HKN) on the north coast of New Britain is reached via a domestic Air Niugini flight from Port Moresby (POM), which is the international gateway to Papua New Guinea.

siteSpecificGear.name: Reef hook
siteSpecificGear.reason: Plateau is exposed; hooking in beats fighting current.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB
siteSpecificGear.reason: Open-water surfacing standard.

name: Inglis Shoals
description: Twin-peaked seamount in the middle of Kimbe Bay covered in pristine hard and soft coral. The peaks rise to 8 m and the swim-through between them at 25 m is the signature feature. Schooling fish and resident reef sharks.
getThere: Hoskins (HKN) on the north coast of New Britain is reached via a domestic Air Niugini flight from Port Moresby (POM), which is the international gateway to Papua New Guinea.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB
siteSpecificGear.reason: Open-water surfacing standard.

name: Dinah's Beach
description: Black-sand muck dive on a gentle slope in Milne Bay, regarded as the birthplace of muck diving along with Lembeh. Mandarinfish display at dusk in the staghorn rubble, and rare nudibranchs are guaranteed. Easy bottom time and zero current.
getThere: Alotau (GUR) is reached via a domestic Air Niugini flight from Port Moresby (POM), which is the international gateway to Papua New Guinea.

siteSpecificGear.name: Macro light
siteSpecificGear.reason: Dusk dives for mandarinfish demand a precise focus light.

name: Black Magic
description: Offshore pinnacle off Normanby Island topped with anemone fields and clouded by clouds of glassfish. The slope drops past 40 m and big-fish action — barracuda, jacks, the occasional hammerhead — works the up-current edge.
getThere: Alotau (GUR) is reached via a domestic Air Niugini flight from Port Moresby (POM), which is the international gateway to Papua New Guinea.

siteSpecificGear.name: Reef hook
siteSpecificGear.reason: Standard procedure on the up-current side.

name: Shark Reef Marine Reserve
description: Famous baited shark dive inside a marine reserve where up to eight species — including bull sharks and the occasional tiger shark — gather. Two-tank format with a deep feed at 25 m followed by a shallow observation dive. Significant adrenaline; structured briefing required.
getThere: Nadi International (NAN) on Viti Levu is Fiji's main gateway, with direct flights from Australia, New Zealand, the US West Coast, and several Asian hubs.

siteSpecificGear.name: Dark wetsuit
siteSpecificGear.reason: Avoid bright colors and contrast on a baited shark dive.

notes: Operator-controlled feed; stay behind the rope line at all times.

name: Cathedral
description: Lagoon bommie with a vaulted swim-through chamber where light beams stream in from above. Surrounded by exceptional Fijian soft coral — Fiji is the soft coral capital and Cathedral is a showcase.
getThere: Nadi International (NAN) on Viti Levu is Fiji's main gateway, with direct flights from Australia, New Zealand, the US West Coast, and several Asian hubs.

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive light
siteSpecificGear.reason: Cathedral chamber is dim inside.

name: Great White Wall
description: Wall in the Somosomo Strait completely sheeted in white soft coral that blooms when the tidal current runs. Entry is through a swim-through tunnel onto the vertical face. The current must be running for the corals to open — timing is everything.
getThere: Nadi International (NAN) on Viti Levu is Fiji's main gateway, with direct flights from Australia, New Zealand, the US West Coast, and several Asian hubs.

siteSpecificGear.name: Reef hook
siteSpecificGear.reason: Once on the wall, hook in and watch the corals open.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB
siteSpecificGear.reason: Drift exits over deep blue water.

notes: Timed to a specific tidal window — operators choose the day, not the diver.

name: Purple Wall
description: Sister wall to the Great White, this section is sheeted in purple soft coral and gorgonians. Same Somosomo Strait current drives the bloom; can be combined with Great White on a two-tank trip. Pristine and rarely crowded.
getThere: Nadi International (NAN) on Viti Levu is Fiji's main gateway, with direct flights from Australia, New Zealand, the US West Coast, and several Asian hubs.

siteSpecificGear.name: Reef hook
siteSpecificGear.reason: Current is the price of admission.

name: Tiputa Pass
description: Channel between the open Pacific and Rangiroa's vast lagoon. Incoming tides funnel grey reef sharks, schooling barracuda, and seasonally pods of bottlenose dolphins that play in the surf. Drift dive on incoming current only; outgoing flow is not divable.
getThere: There are no international flights to Rangiroa — connect through Papeete (PPT) on Tahiti, then take the ~1 hour Air Tahiti hop to Rangiroa (RGI).

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB
siteSpecificGear.reason: Drift exit into the lagoon every dive.

siteSpecificGear.name: Reef hook
siteSpecificGear.reason: For watching the channel mouth action while the current screams past.

notes: Operators run the pass only on incoming tide. Check the tide table when booking.

name: Tumakohua (South Pass)
description: Narrow pass on the south end of Fakarava atoll with a permanent resident population of several hundred grey reef sharks — the famed 'wall of sharks' is biggest in July around the grouper spawning. Easy drift in clean tide-controlled water.
getThere: There are no international flights to Fakarava — connect through Papeete (PPT) on Tahiti, then take the ~1 hour Air Tahiti hop to Fakarava (FAV).

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB
siteSpecificGear.reason: Standard drift surface exit.

siteSpecificGear.name: Reef hook
siteSpecificGear.reason: Hook in mid-channel and watch hundreds of sharks stream past.

notes: Grouper spawning in late June/early July is one of the world's most concentrated shark gatherings.

name: Garuae (North Pass)
description: Widest pass in French Polynesia, 1.6 km across, with strong outflowing currents that bring big-fish action right to the edge. Schools of barracuda, grey reef sharks, and seasonal mantas. Companion site to the more famous South Pass.
getThere: There are no international flights to Fakarava — connect through Papeete (PPT) on Tahiti, then take the ~1 hour Air Tahiti hop to Fakarava (FAV).

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB
siteSpecificGear.reason: Drift exit, often well out into the lagoon.

name: Palancar Reef
description: Cozumel's signature drift along a reef complex of buttresses, swim-throughs, and coral towers that fall away into deep blue. Strong steady current carries divers along while turtles, eagle rays, and splendid toadfish appear in the gaps. The reef stretches for several kilometers and is typically dived as multiple distinct sections.
getThere: Cozumel (CZM) has direct flights from many US cities, or you can fly into Cancún (CUN) and take the 45-minute ferry from Playa del Carmen.

siteSpecificGear.name: Surface marker buoy
siteSpecificGear.reason: Every dive is a drift — SMB required for boat pickup.

name: Santa Rosa Wall
description: Sheer vertical wall plunging from 20 m into the abyss, cut with swim-throughs, tunnels, and overhangs draped in sponges and black coral. The current rides divers along the wall face while groupers, turtles, and the occasional reef shark patrol the blue. Considered one of the finest wall dives in the Caribbean.
getThere: Cozumel (CZM) has direct flights from many US cities, or you can fly into Cancún (CUN) and take the 45-minute ferry from Playa del Carmen.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB + reel
siteSpecificGear.reason: Wall drift ends in open water — operators require SMB on ascent.

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive computer
siteSpecificGear.reason: Multi-level wall profile rewards a conservative computer.

name: Devil's Throat
description: Punta Sur's signature swim-through system — a near-vertical chimney that drops from 27 m and exits into open water on the wall at 40 m. Tight, dark, and unforgiving of buoyancy errors. A bucket-list dive for advanced Cozumel regulars.
getThere: Cozumel (CZM) has direct flights from many US cities, or you can fly into Cancún (CUN) and take the 45-minute ferry from Playa del Carmen.

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive light
siteSpecificGear.reason: Chimney is dark in the middle section even with surface light overhead.

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive computer
siteSpecificGear.reason: Deep profile with no-deco margins that need watching.

notes: Advanced certification + minimum 50 logged dives required by most operators.

name: Dos Ojos
description: Two crystal-clear sinkholes connected by a vast cavern system south of Tulum. Open cavern routes (Barbie Line and Bat Cave Line) keep daylight in view while halocline shimmer and stalactite forests light up under a primary beam. Freshwater, no current, and otherworldly clarity.
getThere: Cancún (CUN) is Mexico's busiest tourist airport, with cheap direct flights from most of North America and Europe. From Cancún it's about an hour's drive south to Playa del Carmen and the main cenote country.

siteSpecificGear.name: Primary dive light
siteSpecificGear.reason: Cavern interior is pitch dark off the guideline.

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive computer
siteSpecificGear.reason: Multiple shallow circuits stack up bottom time.

notes: Cavern-trained guide required. Stay on the line — no penetration past daylight zone without full cave cert.

name: The Pit
description: The deepest known cenote in the Sistema Dos Ojos — a vertical shaft dropping past 100 m with a haunting hydrogen sulfide cloud at 30 m where freshwater meets ancient organic matter. Light shafts pierce the surface and fade into perfect black. Surreal and frequently called one of the most beautiful dives anywhere.
getThere: Cancún (CUN) is Mexico's busiest tourist airport, with cheap direct flights from most of North America and Europe. From Cancún it's about an hour's drive south to Playa del Carmen and the main cenote country.

siteSpecificGear.name: Primary dive light
siteSpecificGear.reason: Below 30 m the hydrogen sulfide cloud blocks all surface light.

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive computer
siteSpecificGear.reason: Deep cenote profile demands tight no-deco tracking.

notes: Advanced + deep cert minimum; tech training recommended. Cavern-trained guide mandatory.

name: Gran Cenote
description: Beginner-friendly open cenote outside Tulum with bright cavern circuits, root-pierced limestone, and resident freshwater turtles in the entry pool. Permanent guideline keeps the cavern route safe for divers new to overhead environments.
getThere: Cancún (CUN) is Mexico's busiest tourist airport, with cheap direct flights from most of North America and Europe. From Cancún it's about an hour's drive south to Playa del Carmen and the main cenote country.

siteSpecificGear.name: Primary dive light
siteSpecificGear.reason: Cavern beyond the daylight zone is fully dark.

name: Great Blue Hole
description: Cousteau's famous circular sinkhole, 300 m across and 125 m deep, off Lighthouse Reef. The dive descends past 30 m where massive stalactites lean from the rim — relics of a dry cave that collapsed when sea levels rose. More geology trip than reef dive; the deep, dark profile is the draw.
getThere: Belize City (BZE / Philip Goldson International) is the country's only international airport, with direct flights from major US hubs. Most divers connect onward by small plane to San Pedro (SPR) on Ambergris Caye or board a liveaboard from Belize City.

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive computer
siteSpecificGear.reason: Deep no-deco profile with a tight ascent margin.

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive light
siteSpecificGear.reason: Stalactite chamber sits in deep shadow below 30 m.

notes: Once-in-a-lifetime geology dive — accept that marine life is sparse below the rim.

name: Half Moon Caye Wall
description: Vertical wall on the eastern edge of Lighthouse Reef with massive barrel sponges, black coral, and a steady parade of eagle rays. Top of the wall starts at 8 m and tumbles into 600 m of blue water. Often paired same-day with the Blue Hole.
getThere: Belize City (BZE / Philip Goldson International) is the country's only international airport, with direct flights from major US hubs. Most divers connect onward by small plane to San Pedro (SPR) on Ambergris Caye or board a liveaboard from Belize City.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB + reel
siteSpecificGear.reason: Wall drifts end in open water.

name: Long Caye Ridge
description: Spur-and-groove reef along Long Caye's protected lee, with healthy hard coral cover, garden eels in the sand patches, and resident green moray eels in the swim-throughs. Gentle conditions make it a popular checkout dive on Lighthouse Reef itineraries.
getThere: Belize City (BZE / Philip Goldson International) is the country's only international airport, with direct flights from major US hubs. Most divers connect onward by small plane to San Pedro (SPR) on Ambergris Caye or board a liveaboard from Belize City.

siteSpecificGear.name: Macro camera
siteSpecificGear.reason: Garden eel and spotted drum colonies reward a long lens.

name: The Elbow
description: Southern tip of Turneffe Atoll where two currents converge and pull pelagics in close. Eagle rays in formation, schooling jacks, and the occasional pod of dolphins. The current is the price — typically a fast drift along a wall and out into blue.
getThere: Belize City (BZE / Philip Goldson International) is the country's only international airport, with direct flights from major US hubs. Most divers connect onward by small plane to San Pedro (SPR) on Ambergris Caye or board a liveaboard from Belize City.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB + reel
siteSpecificGear.reason: Drift exit over deep water — SMB required.

name: Black Beauty
description: Wall dive named for the dense black coral trees draping the deeper section. Visibility consistently tops 30 m and the shallow plateau is loaded with sponges and reef fish. Calmer than the Elbow and friendly to less experienced divers.
getThere: Belize City (BZE / Philip Goldson International) is the country's only international airport, with direct flights from major US hubs. Most divers connect onward by small plane to San Pedro (SPR) on Ambergris Caye or board a liveaboard from Belize City.

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive light
siteSpecificGear.reason: Black coral colors pop only under a beam.

name: CJ's Drop-off
description: Sheer wall a short boat ride from East Harbour where the reef shelf falls into deep water within fin-kicking distance of the mooring. Whale sharks pass through the channel in season and resident eagle rays and turtles patrol the wall year-round.
getThere: La Ceiba (LCE) on Honduras's north coast is reached via domestic flights from San Pedro Sula (SAP), Tegucigalpa (TGU), or Roatán (RTB). From La Ceiba, take the Utila Dream Ferry (~1 hour) to Utila.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB + reel
siteSpecificGear.reason: Whale shark encounters often happen on safety stops in open water.

name: Halliburton Wreck
description: 30 m steel cargo vessel deliberately sunk in 1998, sitting upright in 30 m of water with the wheelhouse at 18 m. Heavy sponge growth, schooling horse-eye jacks, and accessible swim-throughs for trained wreck divers.
getThere: La Ceiba (LCE) on Honduras's north coast is reached via domestic flights from San Pedro Sula (SAP), Tegucigalpa (TGU), or Roatán (RTB). From La Ceiba, take the Utila Dream Ferry (~1 hour) to Utila.

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive light
siteSpecificGear.reason: Interior swim-throughs need a primary beam.

siteSpecificGear.name: Wreck-trained guide
siteSpecificGear.reason: Penetration sections require formal wreck training.

name: Mary's Place
description: Roatán's signature dive — a volcanic crack in the reef forming narrow vertical fissures coated in sponges and black coral. Divers swim single-file through the canyons while light shafts pierce from above. Limited access and timed entry protect the fragile site.
getThere: Roatán (RTB / Juan Manuel Gálvez International) has direct flights from several US hubs plus connections via San Pedro Sula (SAP) on the mainland.

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive light
siteSpecificGear.reason: Canyons are shaded — a light brings out sponge colors.

notes: Site is access-controlled; one boat at a time per management plan.

name: El Aguila Wreck
description: 70 m cargo ship sunk as an artificial reef in 1997 and broken into three sections by Hurricane Mitch a year later. Hull resting at 30 m with the bow at 23 m. Resident green moray, large grouper, and dense schools of horse-eye jacks make this Roatán's top wreck dive.
getThere: Roatán (RTB / Juan Manuel Gálvez International) has direct flights from several US hubs plus connections via San Pedro Sula (SAP) on the mainland.

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive light
siteSpecificGear.reason: Interior sections require a primary beam.

name: Wahoo Rock
description: Pacific seamount off Coiba Island drawing big fish to upwellings — schooling hammerheads, whitetip reef sharks, and dense bait balls. Cold thermoclines and current make it a serious dive, but the pelagic action ranks with the best in the eastern Pacific.
getThere: Panama City's Tocumen International (PTY) is a major hub with direct flights from across the Americas and Europe. Coiba is reached via domestic flight or drive to Santa Catalina, then boat.

siteSpecificGear.name: Temperate wetsuit
siteSpecificGear.reason: Thermoclines drop sharply on the seamount.

siteSpecificGear.name: Reef hook
siteSpecificGear.reason: Hooking in at the up-current edge keeps you on the cleaning station.

name: Twin Peaks
description: Two pinnacles rising from a sandy plain inside Coiba National Park. Whitetip sharks rest in the sand channels between the peaks and large schools of snapper and grunts crowd the summit. Visibility is moderate but the fish density is reliably excellent.
getThere: Panama City's Tocumen International (PTY) is a major hub with direct flights from across the Americas and Europe. Coiba is reached via domestic flight or drive to Santa Catalina, then boat.

siteSpecificGear.name: Temperate wetsuit
siteSpecificGear.reason: Pacific upwellings drop temps mid-dive.

name: Salt Pier
description: Massive industrial pier on Bonaire's southwest coast with pylons coated in orange cup coral, sponges, and resident schools of horse-eye jacks. Shafts of light between the pylons make it a photographer's favorite. Shore-accessible when no salt ships are loading.
getThere: Bonaire (BON / Flamingo International) has direct flights from the US East Coast, Amsterdam, and several Caribbean hubs. The island is small enough that any dive shop is a short drive from the airport.

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive light
siteSpecificGear.reason: Pylon shadows hide macro subjects.

notes: Permission required when ships are present — check with operators day-of.

name: Hilma Hooker Wreck
description: 72 m cargo freighter scuttled in 1984 after a drug-smuggling bust, lying on its starboard side between two reefs at 30 m. The mast reaches up to 18 m and the wheelhouse offers safe penetration for trained divers. Shore-accessible from the salt flats road.
getThere: Bonaire (BON / Flamingo International) has direct flights from the US East Coast, Amsterdam, and several Caribbean hubs. The island is small enough that any dive shop is a short drive from the airport.

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive light
siteSpecificGear.reason: Wreck interior is dark.

siteSpecificGear.name: Wreck-trained guide
siteSpecificGear.reason: Penetration past the daylight zone needs training.

name: 1000 Steps
description: Northern shore dive named for the (actually 67) limestone stairs down to the beach. Healthy hard-coral reef with consistent turtle sightings, garden eel colonies in the sand, and the occasional eagle ray cruising the drop-off. Heavy gear hike up after the dive is the catch.
getThere: Bonaire (BON / Flamingo International) has direct flights from the US East Coast, Amsterdam, and several Caribbean hubs. The island is small enough that any dive shop is a short drive from the airport.

siteSpecificGear.name: Surface marker buoy
siteSpecificGear.reason: Boat traffic on the surface during peak season.

name: Mushroom Forest
description: Shallow plateau on Curaçao's northwest tip studded with hundreds of mushroom-shaped star coral mounds, the largest pushing two meters tall. Healthy reef fish density and an adjacent shallow cave (Sponge Forest) often dived as a second tank.
getThere: Curaçao (CUR / Hato International) has direct flights from Amsterdam, Miami, and several other US and Caribbean hubs. The island is compact — dive shops are 15–30 minutes from the airport.

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive light
siteSpecificGear.reason: Adjacent Sponge Forest cave system benefits from a beam.

name: Watamula
description: Drift dive at Curaçao's northwestern tip where the current sweeps past one of the most pristine reef sections on the island. Dense soft and hard coral, large barrel sponges, and consistent turtle and eagle ray traffic. Boat-access only.
getThere: Curaçao (CUR / Hato International) has direct flights from Amsterdam, Miami, and several other US and Caribbean hubs. The island is compact — dive shops are 15–30 minutes from the airport.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB + reel
siteSpecificGear.reason: Drift exit over open water.

name: Tent Reef
description: Sloping reef along Saba's leeward side with a wall section that drops past 30 m. Healthy stony coral, frequent turtle sightings, and the occasional nurse shark sleeping in the overhangs. Often run as the first checkout dive on a Saba itinerary.
getThere: St. Maarten (SXM / Princess Juliana International) has direct flights from North America, Europe, and across the Caribbean. Winair operates the short hop to Saba (SAB), or you can take the ~90 minute ferry.

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive computer
siteSpecificGear.reason: Multi-level wall profile.

name: Diamond Rock
description: Volcanic pinnacle off Saba's northwest coast that breaks the surface and drops to 25+ m. Schooling fish wrap the rock, and currents pull eagle rays and the occasional shark past in the blue. Conditions can change fast — a more exposed dive than the leeward sites.
getThere: St. Maarten (SXM / Princess Juliana International) has direct flights from North America, Europe, and across the Caribbean. Winair operates the short hop to Saba (SAB), or you can take the ~90 minute ferry.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB + reel
siteSpecificGear.reason: Exposed pinnacle — drift exits common.

name: Tiger Beach
description: Shallow sandbank northwest of Grand Bahama famed for predictable, up-close tiger shark encounters at a baited feed site. Operators run kneeling-in-the-sand interactions in 6 m of water with multiple tigers plus lemon and reef sharks circling. Bucket-list shark diving — not coral.
getThere: Freeport (FPO) on Grand Bahama has direct flights from Florida, or fly into West Palm Beach (PBI) or Fort Lauderdale (FLL) and take a charter or ferry across.

siteSpecificGear.name: Chain-mail glove or dark gloves
siteSpecificGear.reason: Operators issue dark-color gloves for the feed — bare skin attracts attention.

siteSpecificGear.name: Surface marker buoy
siteSpecificGear.reason: Open-water transits between dive sites.

notes: Baited shark feed — assess your comfort level honestly. Liveaboard or day-trip from West End.

name: Thunderball Grotto
description: Famed limestone sea cavern used as a James Bond filming location, with a domed ceiling pierced by skylights that beam shafts of light through clouds of sergeant majors. Mostly a snorkel site, but a shallow scuba option is offered at slack tide.
getThere: Exuma International (GGT) on Great Exuma has direct flights from Miami, Fort Lauderdale, Atlanta, and Nassau. Most dive operations are 15–30 minutes from the airport.

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive light
siteSpecificGear.reason: Side chambers off the main grotto are dark.

notes: Enter at slack tide — currents through the swim-throughs can rip on flood and ebb.

name: Danger Reef
description: Shallow Exuma reef famed for resident Caribbean reef sharks that arrive on the boat sound. Operators run a controlled shark interaction in 12 m with a dozen or more sharks circling. Reef fish density is solid even without the shark feed.
getThere: Exuma International (GGT) on Great Exuma has direct flights from Miami, Fort Lauderdale, Atlanta, and Nassau. Most dive operations are 15–30 minutes from the airport.

siteSpecificGear.name: Dark-color gloves
siteSpecificGear.reason: Standard advice on baited shark sites.

name: Bloody Bay Wall
description: One of the most celebrated wall dives in the Caribbean — a near-vertical drop from 6 m into 1800 m, draped in rope sponges, black coral, and massive barrel sponges. Visibility regularly exceeds 30 m and turtles, eagle rays, and reef sharks cycle along the wall face.
getThere: Grand Cayman (GCM / Owen Roberts International) has direct flights from major US East Coast hubs plus the UK. From there, Cayman Airways operates the short hop to Little Cayman (LYB) or Cayman Brac (CYB).

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive computer
siteSpecificGear.reason: Wall profile demands tight no-deco awareness.

name: Jackson's Bight
description: Adjacent to Bloody Bay Wall but with more swim-throughs and chimneys cut into the wall face. Resident green moray, midnight parrotfish, and a famous Nassau grouper aggregation in winter. Often dived as a second tank on a Bloody Bay morning.
getThere: Grand Cayman (GCM / Owen Roberts International) has direct flights from major US East Coast hubs plus the UK. From there, Cayman Airways operates the short hop to Little Cayman (LYB) or Cayman Brac (CYB).

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive light
siteSpecificGear.reason: Swim-throughs and chimneys benefit from a beam.

name: USS Vandenberg
description: 159 m former missile-tracking ship scuttled off Key West in 2009 — the second-largest artificial reef in the world. Massive radar dishes, gun mounts, and accessible interior compartments draw advanced and tech divers. Top of the superstructure at 15 m, deck at 30 m.
getThere: Key West (EYW) has direct flights from several US East Coast hubs, though many divers drive in via US-1 from Miami (~3.5 hours). Miami (MIA) is the cheaper long-haul option.

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive light
siteSpecificGear.reason: Interior compartments are dark and silt-prone.

siteSpecificGear.name: Wreck-trained guide
siteSpecificGear.reason: Penetration sections require formal training.

name: Christ of the Abyss
description: Bronze statue of Christ standing in 8 m of water on Dry Rocks reef in Key Largo, placed in 1965 and now coated in coral and sponges. Easy shallow dive popular with checkout courses and underwater photographers. Inside John Pennekamp Coral Reef State Park.
getThere: Miami (MIA) is the closest major hub, with direct flights from across the globe. Key Largo and the upper Florida Keys are about an hour's drive south on US-1; Fort Lauderdale (FLL) works equally well.

siteSpecificGear.name: Underwater camera
siteSpecificGear.reason: Iconic photo subject — even smartphone housings deliver here.

name: Manta Heaven Night Dive
description: Iconic night dive off Kona's Garden Eel Cove where divers kneel on a sandy bottom and hold dive lights skyward. The lights attract plankton, and reef mantas with up to 4 m wingspans loop in to feed inches above the group. Reliable encounter rate year-round.
getThere: Kona International (KOA) on Hawaii's Big Island has direct flights from the US West Coast, Tokyo, and several other hubs. Most manta night dives launch from Keauhou Bay, ~15 minutes south.

siteSpecificGear.name: Primary dive light
siteSpecificGear.reason: Light is the entire dive — bring a strong primary.

siteSpecificGear.name: Temperate wetsuit
siteSpecificGear.reason: Hawaii water is cooler than Caribbean — night dive amplifies it.

name: Pinnacle Au Au Crater
description: Volcanic pinnacle rising from a sandy plain off Kona's south coast with lava arches, swim-throughs, and a resident garden eel colony at the base. Dolphins and pilot whales pass overhead and the occasional tiger or oceanic whitetip cruises through.
getThere: Kona International (KOA) on Hawaii's Big Island has direct flights from the US West Coast, Tokyo, and several other hubs. Most manta night dives launch from Keauhou Bay, ~15 minutes south.

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive computer
siteSpecificGear.reason: Multi-level pinnacle profile.

name: Pipin
description: Shallow channel inside the Jardines de la Reina marine reserve where Caribbean reef sharks and silky sharks circle in numbers rarely seen elsewhere in the Caribbean. The reserve's strict protection has preserved shark populations at near-pre-industrial densities.
getThere: Havana's José Martí International (HAV) is the main gateway, with direct flights from most of Europe, Canada, Mexico, and a few US cities. US travelers need to comply with current OFAC rules.

siteSpecificGear.name: Dark-color gloves
siteSpecificGear.reason: Shark-density site — standard advice.

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive computer
siteSpecificGear.reason: Multi-tank shark days stack up bottom time.

notes: Liveaboard-only access. Permit-controlled reserve with daily diver caps.

name: Black Coral 2
description: Wall dive on the southern edge of Jardines de la Reina draped in mature black coral colonies and gorgonians. Schools of tarpon hang in the shallows and silky sharks pass through in summer. Healthier coral than almost anywhere else in the Caribbean.
getThere: Havana's José Martí International (HAV) is the main gateway, with direct flights from most of Europe, Canada, Mexico, and a few US cities. US travelers need to comply with current OFAC rules.

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive light
siteSpecificGear.reason: Black coral colors emerge under a beam.

name: Sapata
description: Volcanic pinnacle off Fernando de Noronha's southwest tip with caves, arches, and a steep wall drop. Lemon sharks rest in the crevices, schools of jacks crowd the summit, and resident spinner dolphins frequently pass through. Visibility regularly exceeds 30 m.
getThere: There are no direct international flights to Fernando de Noronha — connect through Recife (REC) or Natal (NAT) on Brazil's northeast coast, then take the ~1 hour Azul or Gol flight to Fernando de Noronha (FEN). Visitor numbers are capped, so book well ahead.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB + reel
siteSpecificGear.reason: Drift exits over open water.

name: Pedras Secas
description: Cluster of volcanic boulders with tunnels and arches forming an underwater maze. Resident green turtles, frequent spinner dolphin passes, and excellent macro in the cracks. Calmer conditions than Sapata make it accessible to less experienced divers.
getThere: There are no direct international flights to Fernando de Noronha — connect through Recife (REC) or Natal (NAT) on Brazil's northeast coast, then take the ~1 hour Azul or Gol flight to Fernando de Noronha (FEN). Visitor numbers are capped, so book well ahead.

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive light
siteSpecificGear.reason: Tunnels and arches benefit from a beam.

name: Cayo de Agua
description: Outer-atoll wall dive on the western edge of Los Roques, with dense coral cover and consistent eagle ray and reef shark passes. The remote location and limited dive traffic keep the reef in unusually pristine shape for the Caribbean.
getThere: Caracas (CCS / Maiquetía) is the main gateway to Venezuela, though direct international service is limited; many divers connect via Panama City or Bogotá.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB + reel
siteSpecificGear.reason: Atoll drift dives end over open water.

name: Madrisquí
description: Sheltered reef inside the Los Roques lagoon system, ideal for relaxed coral and macro work. Resident nurse sharks rest in the sand channels, queen conchs scatter the bottom, and the visibility regularly tops 25 m.
getThere: Caracas (CCS / Maiquetía) is the main gateway to Venezuela, though direct international service is limited; many divers connect via Panama City or Bogotá.

siteSpecificGear.name: Macro camera
siteSpecificGear.reason: Macro-rich sand channels reward close-focus optics.

name: Silver Bank Humpback Swim
description: Permit-controlled humpback whale sanctuary 130 km off the Dominican Republic where North Atlantic humpbacks gather each winter to breed and calve. Operators run in-water encounters by snorkel only — scuba is prohibited in the sanctuary. The most reliable humpback swim in the world.
getThere: Puerto Plata (POP) on the Dominican Republic's north coast has direct flights from the US East Coast, Canada, and several European cities. The Silver Bank liveaboards depart from Puerto Plata harbor.

siteSpecificGear.name: Freediving fins
siteSpecificGear.reason: Long blades close the gap on a moving whale faster than scuba fins.

siteSpecificGear.name: Underwater camera
siteSpecificGear.reason: Bucket-list footage opportunity — wide-angle preferred.

notes: Snorkel-only sanctuary, January through April only. Permit limits keep boat numbers tight.

name: Banana Reef
description: One of the earliest discovered dive sites in the Maldives, named for its curved banana-like shape. Overhangs, caves, and big coral heads attract schooling bannerfish, oriental sweetlips, and patrolling jacks. Easy entry, photogenic terrain.
getThere: Malé (MLE / Velana International) is the Maldives' only international airport, with direct flights from across Asia, the Middle East, and Europe.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB
siteSpecificGear.reason: Drift exits are common; boat traffic requires surface signaling.

name: HP Reef
description: Protected marine reserve also called Rainbow Reef for the dense soft-coral colors blanketing the pinnacles. Strong current funnels through the channel, attracting grey reef sharks and clouds of fusiliers. A swim-through cave at 20 m is the centerpiece.
getThere: Malé (MLE / Velana International) is the Maldives' only international airport, with direct flights from across Asia, the Middle East, and Europe.

siteSpecificGear.name: Reef hook
siteSpecificGear.reason: Hooking onto barren rock in the channel keeps you positioned for shark traffic.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB
siteSpecificGear.reason: Drift exits over deep water mandate a surface marker.

name: Shark and Yolanda Reef
description: Twin pinnacles at the tip of the Sinai peninsula where Red Sea currents converge. Walls drop into deep blue; barracuda tornadoes and jacks school overhead. The wreck of the Yolanda spilled its cargo of toilets and bathtubs across the reef in 1980 — still scattered on the sand.
getThere: Sharm El Sheikh (SSH) has direct flights from across Europe and the Middle East, with most Red Sea liveaboards departing from the marina ~10 minutes from the airport.

siteSpecificGear.name: 5mm wetsuit
siteSpecificGear.reason: Winter water dips to 20C — tropical suits not warm enough Dec-Mar.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB
siteSpecificGear.reason: Strong drift exits at the tip of the peninsula.

name: Anemone City
description: Plateau carpeted in magnificent anemones and their resident clownfish — hundreds of colonies clustered between 12 and 20 m. Frequently paired with Shark Reef as a back-to-back tank. Calm conditions when current is light.
getThere: Sharm El Sheikh (SSH) has direct flights from across Europe and the Middle East, with most Red Sea liveaboards departing from the marina ~10 minutes from the airport.

siteSpecificGear.name: 5mm wetsuit
siteSpecificGear.reason: Winter Red Sea drops below 22C.

name: Jackfish Alley
description: Drift along a sandy slope with a wall on one side and the blue on the other. Bigeye trevally hunt in packs through the alley; whitetip sharks rest on the sand. Two shallow caves break up the wall around 8 m.
getThere: Sharm El Sheikh (SSH) has direct flights from across Europe and the Middle East, with most Red Sea liveaboards departing from the marina ~10 minutes from the airport.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB
siteSpecificGear.reason: Drift dive exit over open water.

name: Shaab Rumi South Plateau
description: Coral plateau jutting into the Sudanese Red Sea at the southern tip of Shaab Rumi atoll. Hammerhead schools cruise the drop-off in deeper water; grey reef and silvertip sharks patrol the reef edge. Cousteau ran his Conshelf II experiment in the lagoon — remnants still visible.
getThere: Port Sudan (PZU) has limited international service — most divers connect through Cairo (CAI), Doha (DOH), or Dubai (DXB).

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive computer
siteSpecificGear.reason: Repeated deep multi-level dives — wrist computer mandatory.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB
siteSpecificGear.reason: Currents push divers off the plateau into open water.

notes: Remote — liveaboard access only. Departures from Port Sudan.

name: Umbria Wreck
description: Italian cargo ship scuttled by her crew in 1940 to keep her load of bombs and munitions from the British. The hull lies on her port side in 5 to 38 m, holds still packed with ordnance. Penetration is rewarding for trained wreck divers.
getThere: Port Sudan (PZU) has limited international service — most divers connect through Cairo (CAI), Doha (DOH), or Dubai (DXB).

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive light
siteSpecificGear.reason: Penetration into cargo holds and engine room — primary light essential.

notes: Live ordnance aboard — do not touch or disturb.

name: Cedar Pride Wreck
description: Lebanese freighter sunk in 1985 at the request of Jordan's late King Hussein as an artificial reef. Lies on her port side between 7 and 25 m. Mast and superstructure are draped in soft corals; glassfish swarm the holds.
getThere: Aqaba's King Hussein International (AQJ) sits right on the Red Sea coast; Royal Jordanian operates daily hops from Amman (AMM), and many divers connect via Amman from Europe and the Gulf.

siteSpecificGear.name: 5mm wetsuit
siteSpecificGear.reason: Winter water in the Gulf of Aqaba sits in the high teens.

name: Japanese Garden
description: Shore-entry reef slope named for the perfectly groomed look of its coral garden. Hard corals build a tiered slope down to 30 m where soft corals take over. Macro life dense in the shallows — frogfish and seahorses regularly spotted.
getThere: Aqaba's King Hussein International (AQJ) sits right on the Red Sea coast; Royal Jordanian operates daily hops from Amman (AMM), and many divers connect via Amman from Europe and the Gulf.

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive light
siteSpecificGear.reason: Spotting cryptic macro under ledges is the whole point.

name: Daymaniyat Aquarium
description: Protected nature reserve archipelago an hour off Muscat's coast. Whale sharks aggregate here in summer to feed in nutrient-rich upwelling. Reef itself is a healthy mix of hard coral, schooling jacks, and resident leopard sharks on the sand.
getThere: Muscat International (MCT) is the main gateway to Oman, with direct flights from across Europe, Asia, and the Middle East. The Daymaniyat boats launch from marinas about an hour north of the city.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB
siteSpecificGear.reason: Boat traffic in the marine reserve — surface signaling required.

name: Brissare Rocks
description: Granite boulder pinnacle north of Mahé covered in soft corals and resident schooling fish. Whitetip reef sharks rest in the cracks; eagle rays patrol the deeper sides. Granite topography gives it a different feel from typical Indian Ocean coral dives.
getThere: Mahé (SEZ / Seychelles International) is the country's only international airport, with direct flights from the Middle East, Europe, and a handful of African and Asian hubs.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB
siteSpecificGear.reason: Open-water exits common; current pushes around the pinnacle.

name: Shark Bank
description: Offshore granite outcrop rising from 30 m to 8 m below the surface. Top draws schooling yellowtail barracuda and snapper; deeper edges bring eagle rays and the occasional whale shark on the seasonal migration. Current can be ripping.
getThere: Mahé (SEZ / Seychelles International) is the country's only international airport, with direct flights from the Middle East, Europe, and a handful of African and Asian hubs.

siteSpecificGear.name: Reef hook
siteSpecificGear.reason: Strong current on top of the bank — hooking in is the only way to stay put.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB
siteSpecificGear.reason: Drift exits over very deep water.

name: Manta Reef
description: Reef manta cleaning station 22 km offshore from Tofo. Mantas hover at coral bommies while butterflyfish work them over. Often dived in conjunction with surface time spent searching for whale sharks in the bait-rich waters above.
getThere: Inhambane (INH) has domestic flights from Maputo and Johannesburg. Tofo is about a 30-minute drive from the airport.

siteSpecificGear.name: 5mm wetsuit
siteSpecificGear.reason: Mozambique winter (Jun-Aug) drops water below 23C.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB
siteSpecificGear.reason: Surface intervals over deep water mean strong drift potential.

name: Giants Castle
description: Deep reef pinnacle northeast of Tofo Beach famous as a smalleye stingray aggregation site — the world's largest sting ray, almost endemic to this stretch of Mozambique coast. Bull sharks and big groupers patrol the outer wall.
getThere: Inhambane (INH) has domestic flights from Maputo and Johannesburg. Tofo is about a 30-minute drive from the airport.

siteSpecificGear.name: 5mm wetsuit
siteSpecificGear.reason: Winter dives sit at 21-22C — tropical suit insufficient.

siteSpecificGear.name: Computer
siteSpecificGear.reason: Deep multi-level dive — proper computer required.

name: Cathedral
description: Sandstone amphitheater in the middle of Aliwal Shoal — winter aggregation site for ragged-tooth (sand tiger) sharks. Dozens stack inside the bowl from June through November. Tiger sharks are commonly spotted in summer baited dives on the same reef.
getThere: Durban's King Shaka International (DUR) is the closest major airport, with domestic flights from Johannesburg and Cape Town plus some regional international service. Aliwal Shoal is about 90 minutes south by car.

siteSpecificGear.name: 5mm wetsuit
siteSpecificGear.reason: Winter shark season sits at 18-20C.

siteSpecificGear.name: Hood
siteSpecificGear.reason: Backline launches and cold winter water — hood adds critical warmth.

notes: Access via surf-launch Zodiac from Umkomaas — physically demanding.

name: Raggie Cave
description: Series of overhangs where ragged-tooth sharks rest mid-water during their winter mating aggregation. You can drift slowly along the ledge and watch dozens of sharks at arm's length. Whale and dolphin sounds often audible during the sardine run.
getThere: Durban's King Shaka International (DUR) is the closest major airport, with domestic flights from Johannesburg and Cape Town plus some regional international service. Aliwal Shoal is about 90 minutes south by car.

siteSpecificGear.name: 5mm wetsuit
siteSpecificGear.reason: Cold winter water.

name: Two-Mile Reef
description: Southernmost coral reef on the African continent. Hard and soft corals support a teeming reef-fish community plus turtles and the occasional whale shark. Site is huge — operators run different drop points at Anton's, Caves and Overhangs.
getThere: Richards Bay or Durban (DUR) on the KwaZulu-Natal coast are the closest airports, with domestic flights from Johannesburg and Cape Town. Sodwana Bay is about a 3–4 hour drive north along the N2.

siteSpecificGear.name: 5mm wetsuit
siteSpecificGear.reason: Winter water cools below 23C.

name: Five-Mile Reef
description: Less-visited reef north of Two-Mile with deeper bommies and more pelagic action. Big potato bass cruise the gullies; eagle rays and the occasional manta drift over the top. Longer boat ride keeps crowds down.
getThere: Durban's King Shaka International (DUR) is the closest major airport, with domestic flights from Johannesburg and Cape Town plus some regional international service. Aliwal Shoal is about 90 minutes south by car.

siteSpecificGear.name: 5mm wetsuit
siteSpecificGear.reason: Winter water dips into the low 20s.

name: Nosy Tanikely
description: Protected marine park off Madagascar's northwest coast with calm conditions and a relaxed reef community of turtles, octopus, and big honeycomb morays. Easy slope to 20 m suitable for new divers; the macro hunting is excellent.
getThere: Nosy Be (NOS / Fascene) has seasonal direct flights from Paris, Milan, and Johannesburg, plus domestic connections from Antananarivo (TNR).

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive light
siteSpecificGear.reason: Hunting macro under ledges and at dusk.

name: Silfra Fissure
description: Crack between the North American and Eurasian tectonic plates filled with crystal-clear glacial meltwater. Visibility regularly exceeds 80 m. You drift between two continents — at points the gap is narrow enough to touch both walls.
getThere: Keflavík (KEF) is Iceland's only major international airport, with extensive direct service from North America and Europe. Þingvellir is a ~45 minute drive from Reykjavík.

siteSpecificGear.name: Drysuit
siteSpecificGear.reason: 2-4C glacial water — wetsuit is unsafe. PADI Dry Suit cert required by operators.

siteSpecificGear.name: Hood
siteSpecificGear.reason: Exposed face and head freeze in seconds without insulation.

siteSpecificGear.name: Computer
siteSpecificGear.reason: Cold-water dive computer to monitor exposure.

notes: Drysuit certification mandatory. The water is potable — open your reg and drink straight from the source.

name: Blue Hole and Inland Sea
description: Limestone shaft on Gozo's western cliffs leading through an arch into open water at 18 m. Adjacent Inland Sea tunnel cuts through the cliff for 80 m of overhead environment. Both dives sit beside the now-collapsed Azure Window site.
getThere: Malta International (MLA) has extensive direct service from across Europe. The island is small — Gozo and the main Malta dive sites are within an hour's drive plus ferry.

siteSpecificGear.name: 5mm wetsuit
siteSpecificGear.reason: Mediterranean water cold half the year — 14-19C is the norm outside high summer.

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive light
siteSpecificGear.reason: Inland Sea tunnel is a true overhead environment.

name: Azure Window Collapse
description: Iconic limestone arch collapsed into the sea in 2017, leaving an underwater field of car-sized boulders between 15 and 50 m. Now one of the more dramatic geology dives in the Med. The collapsed pillar stands like a fallen monument on the bottom.
getThere: Malta International (MLA) has extensive direct service from across Europe. The island is small — Gozo and the main Malta dive sites are within an hour's drive plus ferry.

siteSpecificGear.name: 5mm wetsuit
siteSpecificGear.reason: Cold Mediterranean outside high summer.

name: Um El Faroud Wreck
description: Libyan oil tanker scuttled off Malta in 1998 as an artificial reef. Lies on her keel at 36 m with the wheelhouse at 18 m. The wreck split in half during a 2006 storm, opening a swim-through that splits the bow from the stern.
getThere: Malta International (MLA) has extensive direct service from across Europe. The island is small — Gozo and the main Malta dive sites are within an hour's drive plus ferry.

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive light
siteSpecificGear.reason: Penetration through holds and engine room.

siteSpecificGear.name: 5mm wetsuit
siteSpecificGear.reason: Cold water majority of year; deep wreck colder still.

name: B-17 Bomber Wreck
description: American B-17 Flying Fortress that ditched off the island of Vis in 1944 after a bombing run over Austria. Sits upright on the sand at 72 m — tech-only depth. Engines, fuselage, and machine guns all intact and recognisable.
getThere: Split (SPU) is Croatia's main Dalmatian gateway, with extensive direct service from across Europe (heavily seasonal). Catamaran ferries from Split harbor reach Vis in about 2.5 hours.

siteSpecificGear.name: Drysuit
siteSpecificGear.reason: Bottom temp at 72 m stays cold year-round; deco hangs at 6 m demand insulation.

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive light

name: Stenjak Cave
description: Limestone cavern carved into the cliff at Stenjak point with a vertical chimney that surfaces inside an air-filled dome. Sunlight beams through the cavern entrance create an Adriatic light show. Resident lobsters and conger eels in the back chambers.
getThere: Split (SPU) is Croatia's main Dalmatian gateway, with extensive direct service from across Europe (heavily seasonal). Catamaran ferries from Split harbor reach Vis in about 2.5 hours.

name: Medes Islands Reserve
description: Strict marine reserve off the Costa Brava since 1983. Decades of protection produced fish stocks unmatched on the Mediterranean coast — clouds of damselfish, big groupers within arm's reach, and snapper schools that pile up around you on safety stops.
getThere: Barcelona–El Prat (BCN) is the main international gateway for the Costa Brava, with direct flights from across Europe and seasonal long-haul service. Rent a car or take a regional train north along the coast to L'Estartit (~2 hours).

siteSpecificGear.name: 5mm wetsuit
siteSpecificGear.reason: Mediterranean water cold most of the year.

siteSpecificGear.name: Hood

name: Princess Alice Bank
description: Underwater seamount 50 nautical miles southwest of Faial. Top rises to 35 m below the surface; pelagic action in mid-water rivals any seamount on Earth. Mobula ray schools number into the hundreds at peak season; blue sharks and mako prowl the deeper sides.
getThere: Ponta Delgada (PDL) on São Miguel is the main Azores gateway, with direct flights from Lisbon and several European and US East Coast hubs. SATA Air Açores connects between the islands.

siteSpecificGear.name: 5mm wetsuit
siteSpecificGear.reason: Open Atlantic stays cool even in summer.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB

name: Formigas Islets
description: Remote pinnacle chain between São Miguel and Santa Maria. Steep walls drop into blue water hosting passing whale sharks, mobula schools, and large dusky grouper. The Dollabarat seamount nearby tops out at 5 m and pulls in pelagic action.
getThere: Ponta Delgada (PDL) on São Miguel is the main Azores gateway, with direct flights from Lisbon and several European and US East Coast hubs. SATA Air Açores connects between the islands.

siteSpecificGear.name: 5mm wetsuit
siteSpecificGear.reason: Atlantic temperatures even in peak season.

name: Cod Hole
description: Far northern Great Barrier Reef site where divers come face to face with potato cod the size of small cars. Years of friendly interaction means the resident pack greets boats. Surrounding bommies host the full Coral Sea reef community.
getThere: Cairns (CNS) is the standard gateway to the northern Great Barrier Reef, with direct domestic flights from all Australian capitals plus regional international service from Asia.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB
siteSpecificGear.reason: Liveaboard drift exits over deep reef.

name: Ningaloo Whale Shark Encounter
description: Snorkel-only encounter with whale sharks that aggregate along Ningaloo Reef from March to August. Spotter planes vector boats onto sharks; you slip in alongside them for a few minutes before they cruise on. Strictly regulated for shark welfare.
getThere: Learmonth (LEA / Exmouth) has direct domestic flights from Perth (PER). Exmouth town and the Ningaloo dive operators are a 30-minute drive from the airport.

siteSpecificGear.name: Snorkel skills
siteSpecificGear.reason: Encounter is snorkel-only by regulation — no SCUBA allowed near whale sharks here.

name: Navy Pier Exmouth
description: Active naval pier converted into one of the highest-density biomass dive sites in the world. Grouper, wobbegongs, and resident grey nurse sharks shelter under the structure; macro life in the pylons rivals Lembeh. Access controlled by the Australian Defence Force.
getThere: Learmonth (LEA / Exmouth) has direct domestic flights from Perth (PER). Exmouth town and the Ningaloo dive operators are a 30-minute drive from the airport.

siteSpecificGear.reason: Under-pier visibility is dim; macro hunting under girders.

siteSpecificGear.name: 5mm wetsuit
