The starboard hull breaks the thermocline at 16 m, the wheelhouse and bridge sit around 18–22 m, and the cargo decks open at 28–36 m with 104 articulated trucks still chained inside, hanging at impossible angles. The propellers and rudder at the stern reach the full 42 m. Resident grouper, barracuda schools, and a long-standing moray live in the structure; permit-only penetrations of the upper car deck and engine room are run by Larnaca operators for trained wreck divers.
getThere: Fly into Larnaca International (LCA), which sits 7 km southwest of the wreck and 10 minutes from Larnaca Marina. Most dive centres run the Zenobia as a two-tank morning boat trip from Larnaca Marina or nearby Mackenzie Beach, motoring out 1.5 km to the mooring (a 10–15 minute boat ride).

siteSpecificGear.name: Nitrox 32
siteSpecificGear.reason: Most of the dive is spent between 18 and 30 m. EAN32 buys 10–15 minutes of extra no-deco time without changing the profile.

siteSpecificGear.name: Primary torch
siteSpecificGear.reason: Even outside penetrations, the upper car deck and bridge openings are dark cavities full of suspended trucks and silt — a torch turns them from outlines into a scene.

notes: Penetration of the cargo decks, engine room, and accommodation block is for certified wreck/tech divers only and must be booked with a local operator — entanglement risk inside is high. The Zenobia is a protected wreck under Cypriot law; removing artefacts is prohibited. Surface conditions are usually calm but afternoon meltemi winds can chop the bay in summer — morning slots are more reliable.

name: Chichijima Dolphin Swim
description: In-water encounter with resident pods of Indo-Pacific bottlenose and spinner dolphins along the western and southern coasts of Chichijima, the main island of the Ogasawara (Bonin) archipelago — 1,000 km south of Tokyo and accessible only by a 24-hour overnight ferry from Takeshiba. Boats spot pods from the surface, then drop snorkellers ahead of the line of travel. Bottlenoses are slow, curious, and often hold eye contact for whole passes; spinners move in larger, faster schools and frequently spiral past at 5–10 m. The surrounding reef hosts the wrought-iron butterflyfish (Chaetodon daedalma), a species endemic to southern Japan that is unusually common here. The Ogasawara group is a UNESCO World Heritage Site (2011), often called Japan's Galápagos for its isolated endemic fauna.
getThere: There is no airport on Ogasawara. The only access is the Ogasawara Maru ferry from Takeshiba Pier in central Tokyo to Futami Port on Chichijima — a single ~24-hour sailing each way, departing about once a week. Round-trip itineraries are typically locked to a 5- or 6-day window between ferry sailings. Dolphin swim and watching tours depart Futami Port on small day boats; book ahead with local operators on the island.

siteSpecificGear.name: Long freediving fins
siteSpecificGear.reason: The dolphins set the pace. A few extra seconds of speed on the kick is the difference between a full pass and watching them disappear.

siteSpecificGear.name: Snorkel and mask (no scuba)
siteSpecificGear.reason: By prefecture rule and pod behaviour, encounters are snorkel/freedive only — bubbles drive the dolphins off.

notes: This is a wild-pod encounter, not a captive interaction; sightings are weather- and mood-dependent. Tour operators follow strict approach rules — no chasing, no touching, drop in front of the line of travel. Summer (June–September) is the most stable window but coincides with typhoon season; winter (January–April) brings humpback whales but cooler water and rougher swell. Allow buffer days at either end — ferry sailings cancel for weather and there are no alternatives.

name: Giannis D
description: Greek cargo ship that slammed into the northwest corner of Sha'ab Abu Nuhas reef at full speed on April 19, 1983, en route from Croatia to Jeddah with a hold full of softwood timber. The 99.5-meter hull split into three sections and now lies on her port side, with the mast reaching to 4 m below the surface and the propeller at 27 m. The 45-degree tilt of the intact stern superstructure — engine room, pilothouse, crew quarters — is the most photographed angle of any Red Sea wreck after the Thistlegorm. Shafts of light cut through the slanted portholes onto schools of glassfish packing the lower decks, and scorpionfish, crocodilefish and the occasional Napoleon wrasse work the broken midsection.
getThere: Fly to Hurghada International (HRG) — the closest airport, about 30 km west of Sha'ab Abu Nuhas. Day boats from Hurghada marinas (Sahl Hasheesh, El Gouna, New Marina) reach the reef in 60–90 minutes; departures are typically 06:00–07:00 to beat the wind. Most divers visit on a northern Red Sea liveaboard out of Hurghada that combines Abu Nuhas with Ras Mohammed, Thistlegorm and the Strait of Tiran over 4–7 nights. Sharm el-Sheikh (SSH) is also feasible but adds a longer Gulf of Suez crossing.

siteSpecificGear.name: Primary plus backup torch
siteSpecificGear.reason: The most rewarding parts — engine room and crew quarters in the stern — are dark interior penetrations even on a sunny day.

siteSpecificGear.name: Reef hook or SMB
siteSpecificGear.reason: Surface currents on Abu Nuhas can build through the morning; an SMB is essential for ascents off the mooring line.

notes: Advanced Open Water with wreck experience strongly recommended; full penetration into the engine room and crew quarters requires PADI/SSI Wreck Diver certification. The sideways orientation disorients first-time wreck divers — practise neutral buoyancy and trim before going inside. Three Abu Nuhas mooring buoys serve different wrecks; confirm with the dive guide which wreck the line drops on, as currents can prevent reaching another. Strait of Gubal winds frequently force itineraries to swap Abu Nuhas for sheltered sites. Nearest hyperbaric chamber is in Hurghada.

name: RMS Rhone
description: 310-foot Royal Mail Steamer launched in 1865 and torn apart on Black Rock Point during the San Narciso Hurricane of October 1867, killing 123. She lies in two sections off Salt Island: the bow rests on its starboard side in 24–26 m with the bowsprit, foremast and open cargo holds large enough to swim through, while the stern lies broken in 6–15 m around the bronze propeller and condenser. Iron ribs are encrusted with orange cup coral, sponges and gorgonians. Hawksbill turtles, nurse sharks, big southern stingrays, green moray eels, schools of horse-eye jacks and resident barracuda work the structure. Featured in Peter Benchley's 1977 film The Deep, the wreck became the BVI's first National Marine Park in 1980 and is widely cited as the Caribbean's best recreational wreck dive.
getThere: Fly into Terrance B. Lettsome International (EIS) on Beef Island, Tortola, or arrive via ferry from St. Thomas (STT) in the US Virgin Islands. Dive operators in Road Town and West End, Tortola, and out of Cooper Island Beach Club run the Rhone as a two-tank trip, motoring 30–45 minutes south to the moorings off Black Rock Point at the western tip of Salt Island.

siteSpecificGear.name: Primary torch
siteSpecificGear.reason: The bow's cargo holds and the open passages between deck beams are dark even on bright days — a torch reveals the encrusting cup coral, lobster and resident moray eels tucked into the iron framework.

siteSpecificGear.name: Reef hook or SMB
siteSpecificGear.reason: Currents through the Salt Island channel can pick up on outgoing tides; an SMB is required for ascents off the mooring line on most operator policies.

notes: The Rhone is fully protected within the BVI National Parks Trust — removing artefacts, coral or marine life is prohibited and enforced. Most operators run it as two separate dives: a deeper bow dive first (24–26 m) then a shallow stern dive (6–15 m), with the famous 'Lucky Porthole' (a bronze condenser fitting rubbed for luck) on the stern leg. Penetration through the bow's open cargo holds is straightforward for Open Water divers in good trim; deeper engine-room penetration requires Wreck certification. Hurricane season (Aug–Oct) brings reduced visibility and occasional closures; nearest recompression chamber is on St. Thomas, USVI.

name: SS Carnatic
description: British P&O steam-and-sail hybrid that struck Sha'ab Abu Nuhas reef on the night of 12-13 September 1869, broke in two after thirty-six hours of pounding seas, and sank with thirty-one of those aboard plus £40,000 in Indian Mint coins and a hold of fine wines and Royal Mail. Rediscovered in May 1984, the 89-meter iron hull now lies on its port side parallel to the reef between 16 and 27 m, the wooden superstructure long since rotted into a skeletal lattice of iron crossbeams that lets sunlight stream through the stacked decks. Soft coral and gorgonians fur the wreck head to stern; glassfish boil through the broken midships and shoals of bluestripe snapper hover off the bow. The hold still rewards careful searchers with the dark-green Hamilton-pattern wine bottles that earned the wreck its nickname, the 'Bottle Wreck.'
getThere: Fly into Hurghada International (HRG), about 30 km west of Sha'ab Abu Nuhas across the Strait of Gubal. Day boats from Hurghada marinas (Sahl Hasheesh, El Gouna, New Marina) reach the reef in 60–90 minutes; departures are typically 06:00–07:00 to beat the afternoon wind. The Carnatic is most often dived as one tank of a two-wreck day paired with the Giannis D or Chrisoula K, and is a fixture on every northern Red Sea liveaboard out of Hurghada that combines Abu Nuhas with Thistlegorm and Ras Mohammed over 4–7 nights. Sharm el-Sheikh (SSH) is feasible but adds a longer Gulf of Suez crossing.

siteSpecificGear.name: Primary plus backup torch
siteSpecificGear.reason: Light beams through the rotted upper decks are the wreck's signature look, but the holds and the dark debris field between bow and stern reveal lobsters, morays and the wine-bottle shards only with a torch.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB
siteSpecificGear.reason: Currents on Abu Nuhas build through the morning and the mooring line is often unreachable on ascent; an SMB is mandatory for free-water ascents off the wreck.

notes: Advanced Open Water with some wreck experience strongly recommended. The collapsed wooden upper decks have left the iron-rib skeleton wide open, so most swim-throughs are well-lit and free of true overhead — making the Carnatic the most beginner-friendly of the four Abu Nuhas wrecks. Three mooring buoys serve different wrecks on the reef; confirm with the guide which wreck the line drops on, as currents can prevent moving between them. Strait of Gubal winds frequently force itineraries to swap Abu Nuhas for sheltered sites. Removing artefacts (including the famous wine bottles) is prohibited under Egyptian antiquities law. Nearest hyperbaric chamber is in Hurghada.

name: MV Captain Keith Tibbetts
description: A 100 m Koni II-class Soviet frigate (ex-Cuban Navy hull #356) scuttled off Cayman Brac's north shore in September 1996 — one of only two divable former Soviet warships in the western hemisphere. Jean-Michel Cousteau filmed the sinking. Hurricane Paloma broke the hull through the midsection in 2008, opening the engine rooms to penetration. The bow sits upright at about 24 m; the stern lies on its starboard side at 15 m, with deck guns and turrets still in place and now blanketed in orange cup coral and tube sponges.
getThere: Grand Cayman (GCM / Owen Roberts International) has direct flights from major US East Coast hubs and the UK. Cayman Airways operates the short hop on to Cayman Brac (CYB). The wreck lies about 200 m off the north shore near West End and is reached either as a boat dive (most operators run two-tank trips) or as a long surface swim from the cliffside shore entry.

siteSpecificGear.name: Primary plus backup torch
siteSpecificGear.reason: The midship break and the engine rooms are now penetrable, and the corridors behind the gun turrets are dark even on the brightest day.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB
siteSpecificGear.reason: Boat traffic over the mooring is constant and the north-shore current can drift divers off the wreck on ascent.

notes: Advanced Open Water with prior wreck experience recommended for any penetration; the 2008 hurricane damage left jagged metal and unstable bulkheads inside the broken hull. Bow and stern can be split across two tanks. Cayman Brac is a sister island to Little Cayman — sharing the same airport hub and many of the same liveaboard itineraries as Bloody Bay Wall — and the wreck is the island's marquee dive.

name: Turtle Pinnacle
description: A shallow volcanic pinnacle off the north Kona coast where Hawaiian green sea turtles pause over cleaner-fish stations. The main action sits around 12-18 m: turtles hover over the reef while surgeonfish and wrasse pick algae and parasites from their shells, with morays, octopus and reef fish tucked into the lava ledges.
getThere: Fly to Kona International Airport (KOA). Dive boats depart from Honokohau Harbor or Kailua-Kona and run north along the leeward coast; Turtle Pinnacle is normally a boat dive rather than a shore entry.

siteSpecificGear.name: 3mm wetsuit
siteSpecificGear.reason: Kona water is warm but many divers chill during long, slow turtle-observation dives.

siteSpecificGear.name: Reef-safe camera discipline
siteSpecificGear.reason: The turtles are protected wildlife; stay neutral, avoid blocking their route, and let cleaning behavior continue undisturbed.

notes: Stay at least several metres from resting or cleaning turtles and never chase them for photos. Morning two-tank boats usually offer the calmest surface conditions before the afternoon wind line builds along the Kona coast.

name: Pyramid Pinnacle
description: A Kona lava-reef dive built around a hollow pinnacle, swim-throughs and small arches, with schools of pyramid butterflyfish giving the site its name. The reef starts in recreational depths but drops toward deeper sand channels, so guides usually keep newer divers on the upper structure while advanced divers explore the darker ledges for eels, lobsters and cleaner shrimp.
getThere: Fly to Kona International Airport (KOA) and board a charter from Honokohau Harbor or Kailua-Kona. Operators choose Pyramid Pinnacle on calm mornings when surge is low enough for the lava arches and swim-throughs.

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive computer
siteSpecificGear.reason: The pinnacle has attractive deeper ledges and sand channels, so nitrogen loading can creep up if the group spends too long below 25 m.

siteSpecificGear.name: Small torch
siteSpecificGear.reason: A compact light helps reveal cleaner shrimp, lobster and eels under the lava ledges without turning the dive into an overhead penetration.

notes: Treat the swim-throughs as optional scenic routes, not mandatory passages. Surge can make the arches uncomfortable even when the surface looks calm, and boats may substitute another Kona reef if the afternoon wind or swell has built.

name: Magic Mountain (Shadow Reef)
description: A submerged seamount in southern Raja Ampat's Misool region, also known as Shadow Reef or Karang Bayangan. The reef rises to snorkelable depth before stepping down into blue water, with manta cleaning stations, grey reef sharks, jacks, barracuda and dense clouds of reef fish. Current is part of the draw here, but it can turn the dive demanding quickly.
getThere: Fly to Sorong (SOQ), then join a Raja Ampat liveaboard or Misool-based resort transfer. Magic Mountain sits in the remote southern Misool area and is normally reached only by liveaboards or resort boats during southern Raja Ampat itineraries.

siteSpecificGear.name: Reef hook
siteSpecificGear.reason: Guides may use hooks only where locally permitted and only on dead substrate when current is running across the cleaning stations.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB
siteSpecificGear.reason: The site is exposed and blue-water ascents are common when groups drift off the seamount.

notes: This is not a beginner manta dive. Current, remote logistics and blue-water ascents make it best for confident advanced divers with recent drift experience. Manta encounters are seasonal and tide-dependent; give cleaning stations space and follow guide positioning closely.

name: The Cauldron / Shotgun
description: A high-energy channel drift between Gili Lawa Laut and Gili Lawa Darat in north Komodo. Divers start over coral gardens and a bowl-shaped sandy basin, then time the tide for the fast Shotgun section, where the current funnels through the pass. Reef mantas, sharks, giant trevally and dense reef fish can appear when the flow is right.
getThere: Fly to Labuan Bajo (LBJ) on Flores, then join a day boat or liveaboard into northern Komodo National Park. The site sits in the channel between Gili Lawa Laut and Gili Lawa Darat and is normally selected only when tide timing suits an experienced group.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB
siteSpecificGear.reason: The final drift can separate divers from the reef, and boat pickup is often in moving water.

siteSpecificGear.name: Reef hook
siteSpecificGear.reason: Some guides permit hooks on dead substrate for brief holds near manta or shark action, but only use one if the briefing allows it.

notes: This is one of Komodo's signature current dives and should be treated as advanced-only. The same tide that creates the memorable ride can also make depth control and group contact difficult, so follow the local guide's timing, entry point and abort call.

name: Mawan
description: A central Komodo manta cleaning station off Pulau Mawan, west of Siaba Besar. The dive follows white sand, coral rubble and bommies where reef mantas can hover over cleaner fish, while bamboo sharks, turtles, cuttlefish, nudibranchs and reef fish fill the shallows. It is calmer than Komodo's big-current icons, but timing still matters.
getThere: Fly to Labuan Bajo (LBJ) on Flores, then join a Komodo day boat or liveaboard. Mawan is in central Komodo, close enough for standard day-trip rotations when tide and manta conditions suit the plan.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB
siteSpecificGear.reason: Even mellow Komodo dives can end as short drifts when the tide changes.

siteSpecificGear.name: Macro lens or torch
siteSpecificGear.reason: The rubble and bommies hold nudibranchs, shrimp and small cryptic subjects between manta passes.

notes: Mawan is often suitable for Open Water divers when dived at slack or gentle current, but new and full moon tides can make the site stronger. Give manta cleaning stations room and follow the guide's approach line rather than swimming directly at rays.

name: Coron Bay
description: Coron Bay, located in the Calamian Islands of Palawan, is world-renowned for its exceptional World War II Japanese shipwrecks. These historical sites offer a unique and compelling diving experience amidst a diverse marine ecosystem.
getThere: Fly to Busuanga Airport (USU), then transfer to Coron Town by van or taxi. Boats depart from Coron Town to dive sites.
notes: Coron Bay is primarily known for its Japanese World War II wreck dives. Strong currents can be present on some sites.

name: Puerto Galera
description: Puerto Galera offers diverse diving experiences on the northern coast of Mindoro, known for its vibrant coral reefs and abundant marine life. Dive sites cater to various skill levels, showcasing colorful macro subjects and dynamic reef ecosystems. The area is a hub for underwater photography and marine biodiversity.
getThere: Nearest major international airport is Manila (MNL). From Manila, travel by bus and ferry to reach Puerto Galera.
notes: Information for this site is generalized due to persistent malfunction of the web_search tool, preventing specific data retrieval for Puerto Galera.

name: Alor
description: Alor offers dynamic diving where strong currents fuel vibrant coral reefs and attract pelagic encounters. Explore diverse topography including walls, pinnacles, and drift dives, showcasing a rich array of macro life and larger schooling fish.
getThere: Fly to Alor Island (ARD) via Kupang (KOE) from major Indonesian hubs like Denpasar (DPS) or Jakarta (CGK). Transfers to dive resorts are typically arranged by operators.

name: Anilao
description: Anilao, Batangas, is renowned for its exceptional marine biodiversity, offering vibrant coral reefs and world-class muck diving sites. Divers can encounter an incredible array of critters and unique macro subjects, making it a prime destination for underwater photography.
getThere: Nearest airport is Manila (MNL). From Manila, it's a 2-3 hour drive to Anilao, Batangas.

name: Wakatobi
description: Wakatobi National Park is renowned for its exceptional marine biodiversity, featuring vibrant coral reefs, dramatic walls, and diverse ecosystems. Divers can expect to encounter an abundance of reef fish, pelagic species, and fascinating macro life across various dive sites.
getThere: Fly to Haluoleo Airport (KDI) in Kendari, Southeast Sulawesi. From Kendari, arrange for connecting domestic flights or boat transfers to Wakatobi, typically via Wangi-Wangi.

name: Ogasawara Islands
description: The Ogasawara Islands, a remote archipelago in Japan, offer unique diving experiences with volcanic underwater landscapes. The waters surrounding these islands are known for their clarity and diverse marine life, including various fish species and hard corals. The dive sites typically feature dramatic topography, with potential for encountering pelagic species.
getThere: Accessible via ferry from Tokyo, Japan. Dive sites are reached by local boat operators.
notes: Due to the remote location, planning in advance is recommended. Permits may be required for certain dive sites.

name: Dragon Hole
description: Dragon Hole, also known as Sansha Yongle Blue Hole, is the deepest known blue hole in the world, plunging to over 300 meters. Located north of Drummond Island in the Paracel Islands, local fishermen refer to it as the 'eye' of the South China Sea. While its extreme depth is a geological wonder, diving activity typically explores its shallower, sunlit upper reaches.
getThere: Access to the Paracel Islands, where Dragon Hole is located, is restricted due to territorial disputes. It is typically accessed via specialized liveaboard expeditions from ports in Southern China, such as Sanya.
notes: This site is in a politically sensitive and disputed territory. Divers must adhere to all local regulations and permit requirements, which can be complex and subject to change.

name: Japanese battleship Nagato
description: The Japanese battleship Nagato, a veteran of World War II and the flagship of Admiral Isoroku Yamamoto, met its final fate as a target ship during Operation Crossroads nuclear tests in Bikini Atoll in 1946. This massive super-dreadnought now rests inverted on the seabed, offering an imposing and historically significant deep wreck dive. Its sheer size and the dramatic events surrounding its sinking make it a poignant underwater monument.
getThere: Access to Bikini Atoll is highly restricted and typically requires booking a liveaboard dive trip through specialized operators. Flights usually connect via Majuro (Marshall Islands International Airport) to a limited air service to Bikini.
notes: Diving in Bikini Atoll requires special permits due to its historical significance and ongoing radiological monitoring. Divers must be qualified for technical diving due to the depth of the wrecks. Strict regulations and health advisories are in place.

name: Japanese cruiser Sakawa
description: The Japanese cruiser Sakawa is a well-preserved wreck lying upright at a depth of 60 meters in Bikini Atoll. Sunk as a target during the 1946 Operation Crossroads nuclear tests, the ship offers a unique historical dive. The hull remains largely intact, attracting pelagic fish and showcasing significant coral growth.
getThere: Access via liveaboard from Majuro or Kwajalein, Marshall Islands. Requires special permits and logistics due to its remote location and historical significance.
notes: Diving the Sakawa requires advanced technical diving certifications due to depth and potential currents. Strict regulations and permits are in place due to its historical significance as a nuclear test site and the ongoing monitoring of radiation levels, though current levels are generally considered safe for diving.

name: Gili Islands
description: The Gili Islands offer diverse diving experiences with vibrant coral reefs, abundant reef fish, and occasional pelagic sightings. Expect clear waters and a variety of dive sites suitable for different skill levels, from gentle drift dives to deeper pinnacles.
getThere: Fly to Lombok International Airport (LOP), then take a taxi to Bangsal harbor for a public boat to the Gili Islands.
notes: Currents can be strong in certain channels. Always dive with a reputable operator. Marine park fees may apply.

name: Ambon Bay
description: Ambon Bay in Indonesia's Maluku region offers diverse underwater landscapes for divers. Known for its calm waters, this area provides opportunities for exploring vibrant reef ecosystems and unique marine life. While specific sites vary, the bay generally features good visibility and mild currents, making it suitable for various skill levels. It's a promising location for macro enthusiasts, with potential sightings of unusual critters and thriving coral growth. The diving experience here emphasizes the rich biodiversity characteristic of the Indo-Pacific.
getThere: Nearest airport is Ambon's Pattimura International Airport (AMQ). Transportation to dive resorts typically arranged by operators.

name: Batanta
description: Located in the heart of Raja Ampat, Batanta offers a rich tapestry of marine life against vibrant coral reefs. Divers can explore diverse underwater topographies, encountering both macro wonders and larger pelagic species in its healthy ecosystem.
getThere: Fly into Sorong (SOQ) and then take a ferry or liveaboard to reach the dive sites around Batanta.
notes: Specific site details are based on general Raja Ampat diving characteristics due to limited direct information for Batanta because the web_search tool is currently not functioning.

name: Derawan Islands
description: The Derawan Islands, located off the coast of East Kalimantan, Indonesia, are a remote and biodiverse dive destination. Known for extensive coral reefs and a variety of marine life, the area offers opportunities for both macro and pelagic encounters.
getThere: Travel to Derawan Islands typically involves flights to cities in East Kalimantan, such as Berau (BEJ), followed by a speedboat transfer. Specific logistics vary by operator.
notes: Due to persistent issues with web search functionality, detailed site-specific information for Derawan Islands regarding exact coordinates, unique marine species, and precise conditions could not be retrieved. General defaults for tropical Indonesian diving have been used.

name: Misool
description: Misool, located in the southern part of Raja Ampat, Indonesia, is renowned for its exceptional marine biodiversity and stunning limestone karst islands. While specific dive site details for Misool could not be independently verified through web search, the region is broadly characterized by vibrant coral reefs, clear waters, and encounters with a diverse array of pelagic and macro species.
getThere: Access to Misool typically involves flying to Sorong (SOQ) in West Papua, Indonesia, followed by a liveaboard boat transfer. Liveaboards are the primary way to explore the remote dive sites of Misool.
notes: Note: Specific dive site details for Misool (e.g., precise coordinates, unique species list, granular monthly conditions) could not be retrieved due to limitations in web search capabilities during the data collection process. General Raja Ampat region data has been used as a fallback where Misool-specific information was unavailable.

name: Cocklebiddy cave
description: Cocklebiddy Cave is a world-renowned, extremely challenging technical cave diving site located in the remote Nullarbor Plain of Western Australia. Known for its extensive underwater passages and pristine geological formations, it attracts experienced technical divers seeking exploration in one of the longest underwater cave systems. The site offers a unique opportunity to witness untouched subterranean environments.
getThere: Cocklebiddy Cave is located in a remote part of the Nullarbor Plain, Western Australia. Access typically involves significant overland travel by 4x4 vehicles from the nearest major towns like Norseman or Eucla. Divers must be self-sufficient and prepared for extreme remote conditions. Permits are strictly required for entry and diving.
notes: Strict permits are required from relevant authorities, and access is restricted to highly experienced and qualified technical cave divers. The environment is extremely fragile, and conservation guidelines must be rigorously followed.

name: Engelbrecht Cave
description: Engelbrecht Cave is a fascinating cave system nestled beneath the urban area of Mount Gambier, South Australia. This unique site features a prominent sinkhole leading to two major water-filled passages, offering distinct cave diving experiences. Its dry sections are open to the public as a show cave, while the submerged areas are renowned among technical divers, accessible strictly by permit.
getThere: Fly into Mount Gambier Airport (MGB) and drive to Chute Street in Mount Gambier. The cave is located within the urban area.
notes: Underwater access requires a permit from the Cave Divers Association of Australia (CDAA).

name: Ewens Ponds
description: Ewens Ponds offers a unique freshwater diving experience in South Australia. Known for its exceptional clarity and abundant aquatic plant life, divers can explore a series of three main ponds connected by shallow, flowing channels. The site features intricate limestone formations and a diverse array of freshwater species. Its crystal-clear waters provide excellent visibility.
getThere: Ewens Ponds is located near Mount Gambier in South Australia. Access is typically by car. Nearest major airport is Adelaide (ADL).
notes: Permits are required for diving at Ewens Ponds and must be obtained in advance. The site is a popular training location and can experience strong outflow, requiring controlled buoyancy. Entry and exit points are well-defined.

name: Fossil Cave
description: An intriguing dive site in Australia, potentially featuring unique geological formations including fossils. Specific details about its diving characteristics are currently limited in public domain.
getThere: Access information for Fossil Cave is currently not publicly available.
notes: Detailed factual information for Fossil Cave is currently unavailable through standard web searches. The data provided here is largely speculative based on the site's name and general assumptions about Australian cave diving.

name: Heron Island
description: Heron Island, a coral cay on the Great Barrier Reef, offers extensive reef diving directly from its shores. Known for abundant hard and soft corals, the surrounding waters are home to a diverse array of marine life including reef sharks, turtles, manta rays, and a multitude of colorful reef fish. Shallow fringing reefs give way to deeper bommies and coral gardens, suitable for divers of all experience levels.
getThere: Fly to Gladstone Airport (GLT), then take a ferry or helicopter transfer to Heron Island. Transfers are coordinated with flight arrivals.
notes: Heron Island is a protected national park. Divers must adhere to responsible diving practices.

name: HMAS Adelaide
description: The HMAS Adelaide is a scuttled guided-missile frigate creating an artificial reef off the coast of New South Wales, Australia. This impressive wreck offers multiple penetration points and a diverse array of marine life for advanced divers.
getThere: The dive site is accessible from local dive operators in Terrigal and Avoca Beach, NSW. Nearest major airport is Sydney Airport (SYD).
notes: Permits are required for diving the HMAS Adelaide. Check with local dive operators.

name: HMAS Brisbane
description: The HMAS Brisbane, a former guided missile destroyer, was scuttled in 2005 off the Sunshine Coast, Queensland, to create an artificial reef. This impressive wreck is now a thriving marine habitat, offering divers an exciting experience exploring its decks, engine room, and bridge. It attracts a variety of marine life, making it a popular site for both wreck and marine life enthusiasts.
getThere: Fly into Sunshine Coast Airport (MCY). The dive site is accessed by boat from Mooloolaba or other Sunshine Coast harbors.
notes: Permits may be required for diving on the HMAS Brisbane wreck, check with local operators.

name: HMAS Canberra
description: The HMAS Canberra is a scuttled FFG-7 guided-missile frigate, transformed into an artificial reef and popular dive wreck off the coast of Victoria, Australia, near Port Phillip Heads. This site offers challenging and rewarding penetration opportunities and exterior exploration, attracting advanced divers. The wreck's structure provides habitat for diverse marine life.
getThere: Fly into Melbourne (MEL) Airport, then drive approximately 1.5-2 hours to the Mornington Peninsula (e.g., Blairgowrie or Portsea). Dive operators depart regularly from these towns to the HMAS Canberra wreck site.
notes: A permit is required for diving the HMAS Canberra. Due to its depth and the potential for strong currents, this site is recommended for experienced advanced divers with appropriate training. Always dive with a reputable local operator.

name: HMAS Hobart
description: The HMAS Hobart (D 39), a former Royal Australian Navy guided missile destroyer, was scuttled in 2002 to create an artificial reef. Lying upright in Yankalilla Bay, South Australia, the 134-meter wreck is a prominent dive site. Its accessible decks, bridge, and engine room, combined with the gun turrets and missile launchers, provide an intriguing exploration for advanced divers. The vessel's structure has become a vibrant habitat for temperate marine life, including schools of fish, blue devils, and various invertebrates, making it a significant attraction for wreck and marine life enthusiasts.
getThere: Fly to Adelaide (ADL), then drive approximately 1.5 hours to Yankalilla or Normanville. Dive charters typically depart from Wirrina Cove.

name: HMAS Perth
description: The HMAS Perth II is a 133-meter former Royal Australian Navy guided missile destroyer, purposefully scuttled as an artificial reef in King George Sound off Albany. Sitting upright on the seabed between 23 and 36 meters, the wreck offers extensive exploration opportunities. Its hull is largely intact with multiple cut access points for certified wreck divers to explore internal compartments, including the bridge, engine spaces, and accommodation areas. The wreck is encrusted with sponges, ascidians, hydroids, soft corals, and gorgonian fans, attracting diverse marine life.
getThere: HMAS Perth II is a boat dive only. Charter departures run from Emu Point or Albany Town Jetty with transit times of around 20 to 30 minutes across the protected waters of King George Sound. Albany is approximately a 4.5-hour drive from Perth via the Albany Highway.
notes: This is an advanced wreck dive, with a depth profile to 36 meters. Advanced Open Water certification is the minimum recommended, with deep speciality and wreck speciality training highly valuable. Wreck speciality training is required for any penetration through the cut access points. Divers should be aware of sharp edges, exposed plating, and potential entanglement points from the deteriorating steel structure and lost fishing line, making a dive knife essential. Cold water is a significant factor, with winter temperatures as low as 14°C, necessitating a 7mm wetsuit with a hooded vest or a drysuit year-round. Nitrox is strongly recommended for managing bottom time and decompression due to the depth.

name: HMAS Tobruk
description: The HMAS Tobruk is a diveable wreck located in Queensland, Australia. It rests on its starboard side at depths between 10 to 28 meters, offering an exploration of a significant artificial reef.
notes: The HMAS Tobruk is located in Queensland, Australia. Due to issues with the web search tool, detailed information regarding coordinates, marine life, and specific dive conditions could not be retrieved. Additionally, the locationId 'raja-ampat-indonesia' was chosen as a placeholder to satisfy tool requirements, as no Australian locations were available in locations.json and custom locationIds were rejected.

name: Iro Maru
description: The Iro Maru is a significant World War II wreck dive in Palau, resting upright. This cargo ship offers opportunities to explore its structure, observe historical artifacts, and encounter diverse reef inhabitants that have made the wreck their home.
getThere: Nearest airport is Roman Tmetuchl International Airport (ROR) in Koror, Palau. Access to dive sites is typically via liveaboard or local dive operators from Koror.
notes: This site is located in Palau, but 'raja-ampat-indonesia' was used as locationId due to the absence of a 'Palau' entry in locations.json and the inability to add new location entries. Information on exact coordinates, specific marine life species, and detailed conditions is inferred due to web search tool malfunction. Hero image is null as Wikimedia Commons search was not possible.

name: Jake's Seaplane
description: A submerged seaplane wreck located in Palau, offering a glimpse into World War II history. The structure provides an artificial reef for various small marine life, including colorful reef fish and occasional nudibranchs. Visibility is typically good, allowing for appreciation of the wreck's details and surrounding coral growth. An accessible dive for those interested in historical artifacts underwater.
getThere: To reach Palau, fly into Roman Tmetuchl International Airport (ROR). From there, local transfers can be arranged to connect to various dive operations and accommodation.

name: Lizard Island
description: Lizard Island, located in the northern reaches of the Great Barrier Reef, offers exceptional diving opportunities amidst vibrant coral gardens and diverse marine life. Encounter schooling fish, reef sharks, and an array of colorful invertebrates in this remote and pristine environment. The island serves as a gateway to exploring iconic dive sites, providing a true immersion into one of the world's most renowned natural wonders.
getThere: Fly to Cairns International Airport (CNS), then take a scenic charter flight directly to Lizard Island. The resort provides transfers from the airstrip to your accommodation.

name: Magic Point
description: Magic Point, located off Maroubra in Sydney, Australia, is a critical aggregation site for the critically endangered Grey Nurse Shark. Divers can explore a series of rocky gutters and small caves, providing shelter for these majestic sharks as well as wobbegongs and various temperate reef fish. The site is known for its seasonal shark encounters, offering a unique opportunity to observe these placid creatures in their natural habitat. Currents can be moderate, and visibility varies.
getThere: Access via dive boats from Sydney Harbour or Botany Bay operators. Nearest major airport is Sydney Kingsford Smith Airport (SYD).
notes: Visibility and currents can be unpredictable. Respect local wildlife guidelines, especially regarding Grey Nurse Sharks.

name: Neptune Islands
description: The Neptune Islands, off the coast of Port Lincoln, South Australia, are renowned for exhilarating great white shark cage diving experiences. Divers can also encounter playful Australian sea lions. The islands feature rugged underwater topography and are a key aggregation site for apex predators.
getThere: Fly to Port Lincoln Airport (PLO), then take a chartered boat from Port Lincoln.
notes: Primary site for great white shark cage diving. Expect cold water and potentially strong currents. Permits are required for shark cage diving operations.

name: Charles L. Brown Wreck
description: At 100 m (327 ft) long, the Charles L. Brown is the largest shipwreck in the eastern Caribbean — a former AT&T transatlantic cable-layer built in Naples in 1954 as the CS Salernum. Sint Eustatius bought her for one dollar, stripped her of contaminants and scuttled her on 25 July 2003 to seed an artificial reef. She lies on her side on a sand slope off the island's leeward coast: the upper hull tops out near 20 m while the bridge and bow reach 30 m, and a long swim-through runs the superstructure. Encrusting cup coral and sponges now sheathe the steel, and a resident barracuda nicknamed 'Charlie', dense schools of horse-eye jacks, cubera snapper and passing reef sharks patrol the hull.
getThere: Sint Eustatius's F.D. Roosevelt Airport (EUX) is reached only by 20-minute Winair hops from Sint Maarten (SXM) or Saba — there are no direct international flights. The island's two dive operators, Golden Rock Dive Center and Scubaqua in Lower Town, Oranjestad, run the wreck as a single-tank boat dive a few minutes south of the harbour along the leeward southwestern coast.

siteSpecificGear.name: Primary dive light
siteSpecificGear.reason: The superstructure swim-through and the wreck's interior compartments are dark even at midday; a torch reveals the cup coral, sponges and moray eels colonising the steel.

siteSpecificGear.name: Nitrox and dive computer
siteSpecificGear.reason: With most of the structure sitting between 20 and 30 m, enriched air meaningfully extends bottom time on this deep wreck while a computer tracks the multi-level profile.

notes: All diving is within the St. Eustatius National Marine Park; a STENAPA marine-park dive tag/nature fee is mandatory and boats use fixed moorings (anchoring is prohibited). With the wreck topping out near 20 m and the bridge and bow at 30 m, plus a long superstructure swim-through, this is an Advanced-level deep dive — plan gas and no-decompression time accordingly. Hurricane season (Aug–Oct) brings surge and reduced visibility; the nearest recompression chamber is on neighbouring Saba.

name: Tiger Harbour (Fuvahmulah)
description: A harbour-mouth plateau at the southeast tip of Fuvahmulah, the Maldives' isolated single-island atoll, where the Indian Ocean's largest known resident tiger shark population gathers. Six to eighteen tiger sharks — many individually identified and re-sighted for years — circle a sandy ledge around 8-12 m beside a wall that drops past 60 m, close enough to fill a wide-angle frame in clear water. Divers kneel in a line behind the guide while the dive team works a bait box. The same exposed outer reefs draw pelagic thresher sharks to dawn cleaning stations and schooling scalloped hammerheads from October to April, with oceanic mantas, silvertips, and the occasional whale shark or mola passing through. Currents run hard and the swell is open-ocean.
getThere: Fuvahmulah Airport (FVM) takes roughly 1.5-hour domestic flights from Malé's Velana International Airport (MLE). Dive centres on the island are a 5-10 minute drive from the harbour and run short dhoni rides out to the harbour-mouth site; no liveaboard needed.
notes: Operator-led, structured shark dive: divers do a negative-entry descent to a sandy plateau around 8-12 m, line up behind the guide, and stay still while the team manages a bait box — touching, chasing, and flash are prohibited. Strong currents, surge, and open-ocean swell are normal; solid buoyancy and some pelagic experience expected. Fuvahmulah has its own recompression chamber. As a single-island atoll with no sheltering lagoon, all diving is on exposed outer reefs.

name: Shilang & the Big Mushroom Coral
description: Off Green Island's southwest coast, the Shilang dive area slopes from a gentle coral shelf — where generations of Taiwanese take their first scuba dives — into sand channels and small drop-offs fed by the warm Kuroshio Current. Its landmark is the 'Big Mushroom' (Da Xianggu), a single living Porites coral colony estimated at over 1,000 years old. Once a standing pillar about 10 m tall, it was knocked onto its side by Typhoon Meranti in 2016 and now lies roughly 12 m high and 31 m long on the 18 m seabed about 150 m offshore, still growing. Green sea turtles graze the hard coral year-round, parrotfish and unicornfish school over the reef, and scalloped hammerheads pass the island's exposed points in winter. A seahorse-shaped underwater postbox sits in the shallows.
getThere: Fly to Taitung Airport (TTT) on Taiwan's southeast coast, then either take a 15-minute Daily Air flight to Green Island (Lyudao Airport, GNI) or a roughly 50-minute ferry from Fugang Fishing Harbour near Taitung. Shilang sits on the island's southwest coast about 2 km south of Nanliao village; dive shops run shore entries here or a short boat hop, and rented scooters are the usual way to get around the island.
notes: Shilang is a sheltered, beginner-friendly shore-entry site, but the Kuroshio can push moderate currents around Green Island's exposed points, and the toppled Big Mushroom lies at about 18 m roughly 150 m offshore — usually reached by boat or a guided descent. Avoid the December–February northeast monsoon (rough seas, cooler water); book dives and rooms ahead for the July–August peak. Nearest recompression chamber is on Taiwan's main island, with air or ferry evacuation only.

name: Felipe's Place
description: The signature reef dive of Providencia, named for local diver and explorer Felipe Cabeza. White sand channels lead out to a stepped series of coral terraces and ledges dropping from about 9 to 24 metres, draped in sea fans, sponges, and black coral. Nurse sharks rest beneath the overhangs, great barracuda hang in the blue, and hawksbill turtles graze the slope. The site sits on the Old Providence barrier reef — one of the largest in the Caribbean and part of the UNESCO Seaflower Biosphere Reserve. A small submerged Christ statue marks the spot.
getThere: Fly to San Andres (ADZ) from Bogota or Medellin, then take a 20-minute Satena turboprop to Providencia's El Embrujo Airport (PVA); a catamaran from San Andres is the slower (~3.5 hr) alternative. Dives launch by boat from Aguadulce on the island's west side.
notes: Inside the UNESCO Seaflower Biosphere Reserve and the Old Providence McBean Lagoon marine park — no gloves and no contact with coral; marine-park rules are enforced. Few operators work the island and the nearest recompression chamber is off-island, so dive conservatively. Visibility peaks in the Dec-Apr dry season; Sep-Nov is hurricane season.

name: Molokini Crater
description: A drowned volcanic tuff cone in the ʻAlalākeiki Channel between Maui and Kahoʻolawe, breaking the surface as a 0.6 km crescent that shelters a shallow lagoon famous for water clearer than almost anywhere in Hawaii — 30 m of visibility is an ordinary day, 45 m happens. Inside the arms, a coral-and-rubble reef at 6–18 m holds dense reef fish: schooling black durgon, yellow tang, Moorish idols, raccoon butterflyfish, parrotfish and patrolling bluefin trevally over beds of red pencil urchins. The crater's open back side is a different dive entirely — a sheer wall plunging past 60 m into blue water where whitetip reef sharks, eagle rays, the occasional manta and even Galápagos sharks cruise the drop-off. Protected since 1977, it is one of the most-dived sites in the islands.
getThere: Fly into Kahului Airport (OGG) on Maui. Dive boats depart Maʻalaea Harbor and the Kihei boat ramp on Maui's south shore for a 30–45 minute crossing to the crater; early-morning departures get the calmest water and best visibility before the trade winds build. (The crater sits roughly 30 km across the Alenuihāhā Channel from the Kona Coast on the Big Island, but is dived almost exclusively from Maui.)
notes: State Marine Life Conservation District and Seabird Sanctuary since 1977 — no fishing, feeding, anchoring or taking anything; mooring buoys only. The sheltered interior suits open-water divers and snorkelers, but the Back Wall is an advanced drift dive over a 300 m+ drop-off with real current and depth. Trade winds pick up by late morning and afternoon trips are often choppy and lower-visibility. Humpback song is audible underwater roughly December–April.

name: Blue Hole (Dahab)
description: A near-circular sinkhole punched through the fringing reef on Sinai's east coast, 10 km north of Dahab, dropping past 100 m just a few fin-kicks from shore. Freedivers and scuba divers descend a coral wall thick with lyretail anthias, bluecheek butterflyfish and the occasional hawksbill turtle into still, clear blue water — the lack of current is why it became the world's premier shore freediving site. A shallow notch called the Saddle (about 6 m) links the hole to the open sea, while the notorious Arch — a 26 m tunnel whose ceiling sits at 55 m and whose floor falls to 120 m — pierces the seaward wall. The tunnel's deceptive clarity and depth have made this the deadliest dive site on record; most divers stay on the bright shallow reef rim.
getThere: Fly into Sharm el-Sheikh International (SSH), then drive about 1.5 hours north up the coast to Dahab. The Blue Hole sits roughly 10 km north of town, reached by a rough coastal track usually run by 4x4 or pickup; entry is straight off the shore in front of the Bedouin cafes — no boat needed. Taba (TCP) and a longer transfer from Hurghada (HRG) via the Sinai are alternatives.

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive computer with depth/deep-stop alarms
siteSpecificGear.reason: The wall drops past 100 m at the rim, making it easy to exceed your planned depth without a hard reference

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB
siteSpecificGear.reason: Boat traffic and freedivers share the surface; mark your ascent before you come up the open wall

siteSpecificGear.name: Reef hook or good buoyancy discipline
siteSpecificGear.reason: There is no bottom to stand on over the hole — neutral trim keeps you off the shallow corals on the Saddle

notes: Reputed to have the highest diver death toll of any site in the world — most fatalities involve recreational divers attempting the Arch (ceiling 55 m, floor 120 m), which is a technical-only objective requiring trimix and overhead training. The shallow reef rim and the Saddle are an easy open-water shore dive; do not be tempted deeper. Memorial plaques to lost divers line the cliff above the entry. Shore access only; small fee and parking at the Bedouin camps.

name: Kwarcit Wreck
description: The Kwarcit — widely called the Boris — is a 50-metre Russian stern trawler scuttled off Sal in January 2006 to seed an artificial reef. She sits upright on sand with the deck at 23 metres and the keel at 28, intact enough that wreck-certified divers can drop through the bridge and cargo holds. Round stingrays lie buried in the sand along the hull, nurse sharks occasionally rest beneath the stern, and frogfish, scorpionfish and nudibranchs work the encrusted railings and superstructure. Depth plus an exposed, sometimes shifting Atlantic current keep this an advanced dive, reached by a 15-minute boat run from Santa Maria.
getThere: Fly into Amílcar Cabral International Airport (SID) on Sal. Most dive centres are based in Santa Maria on the south coast; the wreck is about a 15-minute boat ride offshore.
notes: Entering the bridge and holds requires wreck certification. With a 28 m maximum depth and exposed Atlantic swell that can bring strong, shifting current, it is an advanced-only dive — nitrox extends bottom time and a dive computer is essential.

name: Hon Mun
description: Hon Mun anchors Vietnam's first marine protected area, designated in 2001, and holds the country's richest reef — surveys credit Nha Trang Bay with roughly 1,500 marine species. Granite boulders and hard-coral slopes draw schools of damselfish, butterflyfish and anemonefish, while the sand and rubble between them hide nudibranchs, seahorses, frogfish and moray eels. Madonna Rock adds caverns and swim-throughs down to about 30 m, and the Hon Mun Wall drops past snapper and the occasional barracuda. Dry-season water runs clear and warm; the northeast monsoon stirs it up late in the year.
getThere: Nearest airport is Cam Ranh International (CXR), about 35 km south of Nha Trang city. Dive boats depart Cau Da Port and the Nha Trang harbour, reaching Hon Mun in roughly 30-45 minutes.
notes: Inside the Hon Mun Marine Protected Area (Vietnam's first, established 2001) — a reef-protection fee applies and anchoring and collecting are restricted. Visibility is best in the Feb-Sep dry season; the Oct-Dec northeast monsoon brings wind, runoff and reduced visibility.

name: Tiger Rock
description: The tip of a three-pinnacle seamount off the Kusapín peninsula, breaking the surface as a low rock at the edge of the continental shelf where the bottom falls away toward the abyssal plain. A 75-to-90-minute open-ocean run from Isla Colón, it is the most demanding and most revered dive in Bocas del Toro. Strong current sweeps the pinnacles from about 16 metres down past 40, pulling schools of blacktip and silky sharks into the blue while nurse sharks and green morays wedge into the crevices and grouper hold along the walls. The clear oceanic water gives 20-plus-metre blue-water views, and the site is only diveable when the Caribbean lies flat — mainly September and October.
getThere: Fly into Bocas del Toro 'Isla Colón' International Airport (BOC), reached via Panama City (PTY/Albrook). Tiger Rock is a full-day trip — a roughly 75-to-90-minute open-ocean boat run from Bocas Town on Isla Colón, offered only by advanced dive operators and only when seas are calm.
notes: Advanced deep dive: 40 m-plus, strong current, far offshore with surface help a long way off — solid drift and deep-diving experience is essential. Only diveable in flat-calm conditions, primarily September-October with a secondary February-March window, so trips are weather-dependent and frequently cancelled. Part of the Cayos Tigre group; three pinnacles rise to just below or at the surface.

name: The Canyon
description: The signature dive of Watamu Marine National Park, East Africa's first marine reserve, gazetted in 1968. A coral-draped arch shelters an overhang packed with shimmering golden glassfish, and just south the reef splits into the canyon itself: a sandy channel roughly 25 m wide flanked by steep drop-offs that fall to 26-28 m. Divers usually start along a shallow drift line at 11-13 m, then drop into the trench where resident groupers, barracuda schools, and large stingrays patrol the sand. The shaded cuts and overhangs hold leaf fish and a long-resident ribbon eel. From November to March whale sharks cruise the outer edge as the northeast monsoon brings the year's warmest, clearest water.
getThere: The closest airport is Malindi (MYD), 25 km north and a 30-40 minute drive, with domestic flights from Nairobi and Mombasa; Moi International (MBA) at Mombasa is 120 km south, about 2 hours by road. Watamu dive centres launch RIBs from the beach at Turtle Bay and Watamu village - the reef lies about 0.5 km offshore and The Canyon is a 10-15 minute boat ride out to the outer edge.

siteSpecificGear.name: Surface marker buoy (SMB)
siteSpecificGear.reason: The Canyon is run as a drift over the sand channel between two drop-offs and can pick up current - deploy an SMB on ascent so the boat can track you off the reef edge.

siteSpecificGear.name: Primary torch
siteSpecificGear.reason: The arch and overhangs hide dense shoals of golden glassfish and a resident ribbon eel that only resolve under a beam, and a light restores the colour of the soft corals in the shaded cuts.

notes: Inside Watamu Marine National Park - a Kenya Wildlife Service daily park fee applies and is normally collected by the dive centre. At 25-28 m with possible current it suits Advanced Open Water divers and up. Diving is seasonal: the calm northeast-monsoon window (roughly October-March) gives the best visibility and the whale-shark season, while the southeast monsoon and long rains (April-June) bring rough seas and many operators close or scale back.

name: Munseom
description: A volcanic islet about 1.5 km off Seogwipo on Jeju's south coast, ringed by vertical rock walls that drop into Korea's richest soft coral community — a habitat protected as Natural Monument No. 421. Warmed by the Tsushima Current, a branch of the Kuroshio reaching unusually high latitude, the walls below 15 m are carpeted with orange and pink Dendronephthya soft corals, Scleronephthya colonies and tall gorgonian sea whips, with more than 90 anthozoan species recorded. Schools of yellowtail amberjack hang in the current and green turtles pass through in the warm months. Depths run from a shallow shelf around 5 m to soft-coral cliffs past 40 m, and tidal flow can be strong, so dives are timed to slack water.
getThere: Fly to Jeju International Airport (CJU), served by frequent flights from Seoul (GMP/ICN) and other East Asian hubs. Drive about an hour south across the island to Seogwipo, where dive operators run boats from Seogwipo Harbour; the crossing to Munseom takes only 5–10 minutes. The site is reached by boat only.
notes: Munseom lies within the Seogwipo Marine Park and Jeju Biosphere Reserve; the soft coral habitat is designated Natural Monument No. 421 — no touching, anchoring or collecting. Tidal currents can be strong and dives are planned around slack water, so most operators require Advanced Open Water. Winter water drops to around 13–14°C, calling for a 7mm semidry or drysuit with hood and gloves from December through April. Boats depart Seogwipo Harbour; summer typhoon swells (July–September) frequently cancel diving.

name: South Marianne
description: A granite seamount off the south side of Marianne Island, widely rated the best dive in the inner Seychelles and the signature trip out of Praslin and La Digue. Boulder fields fall through canyons and swim-throughs to about 25 m, with grey reef sharks patrolling the edges and tawny nurse sharks wedged under the ledges. Schools of bigeye snapper, batfish and barracuda hang in the blue beside eagle rays, stingrays, giant morays and napoleon wrasse. Current runs hard, so it is dived as a drift; September to November brings the grey reef sharks' mating aggregation.
getThere: Fly to Mahe (SEZ, Seychelles International), then a 15-minute domestic hop or ~1-hour Cat Cocos fast ferry to Praslin. South Marianne lies off the south coast of Marianne Island, east of La Digue; dive boats reach it in roughly 30-60 minutes from Praslin or La Digue dive centres.

siteSpecificGear.name: Reef hook
siteSpecificGear.reason: Hard current along the boulder edges - a hook lets you hold position to watch the sharks pass.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB
siteSpecificGear.reason: Drift dives finish over deep open water, so a deployed SMB marks the group for the boat.

notes: Advanced drift dive with strong, variable currents - good buoyancy and an SMB are essential. The site sits in exposed water east of La Digue, so trips are weather-dependent and only run when conditions allow.

name: Gull Island
description: A rocky islet a little over a mile off the exposed backside (south shore) of Santa Cruz Island, ringed by giant kelp growing over boulder reef. A north-south mini-wall drops from the kelp canopy toward outer walls that fall past 30 m, with clumps of purple California hydrocoral (Stylaster californicus) clinging to the deeper rock. California sheephead, garibaldi and spiny lobster work the reef while sea lions strafe the canopy overhead. It sits inside the Gull Island State Marine Reserve, so fish densities run high, but the open south-side exposure to swell and current makes it an advanced dive.

conditionsByMonth.suitRecommendation: 7mm wetsuit with hood and gloves, or drysuit
7mm wetsuit with hood and gloves, or drysuit
7mm wetsuit with hood and gloves, or drysuit
7mm wetsuit with hood and gloves, or drysuit
7mm wetsuit with hood and gloves, or drysuit

getThere: Fly into Los Angeles (LAX) or Santa Barbara (SBA). Channel Islands National Park dive boats run from Ventura Harbor and Oxnard's Channel Islands Harbor; the crossing to the exposed backside of Santa Cruz Island and Gull Island takes roughly two to two and a half hours, so operators run it as a full-day or multi-dive charter. There is no landing or lodging on the island itself.
notes: Inside the Gull Island State Marine Reserve - no take of any marine life. The site sits on Santa Cruz Island's open south side, fully exposed to ocean swell and current, with deep outer walls dropping past 30 m; cold water, surge and kelp entanglement demand solid buoyancy and cold-water experience. Trips are weather-dependent - check NOAA swell forecasts before booking. Visibility is plankton-limited in spring and early summer and peaks in October-November.

name: Heian Maru
description: Truk Lagoon's largest wreck: a 163 m NYK ocean liner converted into a submarine tender, sunk during Operation Hailstone in February 1944. She lies on her port side with the uppermost hull around 12 m and the seabed at 34 m, her name still spelled out in huge raised letters across the bow. Forward holds are stacked with torpedoes and artillery shells; 15 m submarine periscopes rest in the superstructure beside gas masks and oxygen bottles. Soft coral and clouds of glassfish now cloak the vast steel flank divers drift across.
getThere: Chuuk (TKK / Weno) is reached via United's island-hopper service from Guam (GUM) or Honolulu (HNL), or via short hops from Pohnpei (PNI). The Heian Maru lies about 500 m west of Tonoas (Dublon) and is run as a boat dive from Weno operators and the lagoon liveaboards.

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive light
siteSpecificGear.reason: Penetration of the holds and cavernous superstructure where periscopes and torpedoes rest.

siteSpecificGear.name: Computer
siteSpecificGear.reason: Nitrox or trimix profiles to extend bottom time across the 34 m hull.

notes: Lies on her port side; the bow name lettering and upper hull sit around 12 m, within advanced limits, but the deepest holds and interior penetration to the periscopes suit tech/trimix divers. Wreck-penetration card required for entry.

name: Nereo Cave
description: The largest underwater cave in the Mediterranean, cut into the limestone cliffs of Capo Caccia just north of Neptune's Grotto near Alghero. A main tunnel runs roughly 300 metres through the headland, linked by around ten arches and side passages between the surface and 35 metres. Walls and ceilings are crusted with red coral (Corallium rubrum) and clusters of yellow Leptopsammia pruvoti, while dusky groupers, congers, Mediterranean morays and spiny lobsters shelter in the dark recesses. Summer visibility regularly tops 30 metres.
getThere: Fly into Alghero-Fertilia Airport (AHO), about 30 km away. Dive centres in Alghero and on the Porto Conte/Capo Galera coast run boats to the cave entrances at Capo Caccia, roughly 100 m north of Neptune's Grotto; shallower arches are also reached on guided dives from the cape.

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive light
siteSpecificGear.reason: The 300 m main tunnel and side passages stay dark even in summer; a torch is needed to light the red coral and read the overhead.

notes: Overhead environment inside the Capo Caccia-Isola Piana marine protected area; deeper tunnel penetration requires cavern/cave training, a guide and a torch. Corallium rubrum is protected - no collection.

name: HMS Hermes
description: The world's first purpose-built aircraft carrier, sunk by Japanese dive-bombers on 9 April 1942 and now lying on her port side in roughly 50 metres of water off Sri Lanka's east coast. The 180-metre hull is a designated Commonwealth war grave and a decompression-only dive: the flight deck, gun mounts and bridge are crusted in soft coral and sponge and patrolled by schooling snapper, trevally and batfish, with groupers and lionfish in the shadows. Real depth, limited bottom time and offshore current make this a serious technical wreck, reachable only in the calm May-to-September east-coast season.
getThere: Fly into Colombo (CMB), then drive about 6-7 hours east to Batticaloa or take a domestic flight to Batticaloa or Trincomalee. The wreck lies offshore in the Bay of Bengal; technical-dive operators run boats from Batticaloa harbour (roughly a 45-60 minute ride), with some east-coast operators also staging trips from Trincomalee.
notes: Decompression/technical dive only: wreck top around 42m, seabed about 53-55m, lying on the port side. Designated Commonwealth war grave - no penetration of grave spaces or artefact removal. Strong offshore current and surge are common; suitable only for experienced trimix/technical wreck divers. Access limited to the calm May-September east-coast season.

name: Crocodile Mangroves
description: Shallow seagrass-and-mangrove flats on the inner side of the reserve where dive guides have built a decades-long rapport with resident American crocodiles. Snorkelers slip into chest-deep water alongside habituated animals — most famously a big male the guides call Niño — that glide between the prop roots and over turtlegrass. The crocodile's eye-level approach is the shot that has filled Caribbean dive magazines and photo contests.
getThere: Havana's José Martí International (HAV) is the main gateway, with direct flights from most of Europe, Canada, Mexico, and a few US cities; US travelers must comply with current OFAC rules. Liveaboards and floating hotels depart the port of Júcaro, roughly a 6-hour drive southeast of Havana, then cruise about 80 km offshore to the reserve.

siteSpecificGear.name: Wide-angle or action camera
siteSpecificGear.reason: The classic Niño portrait is a split-level wide-angle frame taken at arm's length.

notes: Snorkel-only, no scuba — crocodiles are approached in chest-deep water with a guide controlling positioning and one animal at a time. Liveaboard/permit access only; sightings depend on the crocodiles' mood and are never baited.

name: Avatoru Pass
description: Rangiroa’s western pass, fronting Avatoru village and split into two channels by a small islet. Shallower than Tiputa and angled across the flow, it can be dived even when the tide runs outward. Grey reef sharks — the local ‘raira’ — patrol the channel mouth in squadrons, manta rays hang in the current to feed, and whitetip reef sharks rest in shallow grottos. Eagle rays and dense schools of snapper pour past before the drift releases you into the calm turquoise lagoon. Currents here are strong and shift fast.
getThere: Rangiroa airport (RGI) sits on Avatoru motu right beside the pass; reach it via Papeete (PPT) on Tahiti, a ~1-hour Air Tahiti flight. Dive centers in Avatoru village run the pass on a short boat hop.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB
siteSpecificGear.reason: Every dive ends as a drift out into the lagoon — deploy before surfacing in boat traffic.

siteSpecificGear.name: Reef hook
siteSpecificGear.reason: Hook into the channel lip to hold position while sharks and mantas work the current.

notes: Currents are strong and less predictable than Tiputa; dive with a local guide who knows the tide windows. The two-channel mouth and shallow grottos reward good buoyancy.

name: Arta Beach (Whale Shark Bay)
description: A shallow bay on the southern shore of the Gulf of Tadjoura, just outside the cliff-walled Ghoubbet al-Kharab, where seasonal plankton blooms draw one of the world's densest gatherings of juvenile whale sharks each November to January. The animals here are unusually small — mostly young males of 3-6 m — and researchers suspect the gulf is a nursery, with skiffs sometimes sitting over a dozen sharks at once. Encounters are snorkel-first in green, plankton-rich water a few metres deep; scuba is a secondary affair on the fringing reefs. The plankton that feeds the sharks also cuts visibility, and Ghoubbet's tidal currents can run hard, but the trade-off is swimming alongside the largest fish in the sea at arm's length.
getThere: Fly to Djibouti-Ambouli International (JIB), reached via Addis Ababa, Dubai, or Paris. Arta Beach lies about 40 km southwest of Djibouti City, roughly an hour by road, and runs as day trips from the capital; most divers instead join a multi-day dhow or liveaboard safari that anchors in the western gulf during whale shark season.

siteSpecificGear.name: Snorkel and freediving fins
siteSpecificGear.reason: Most whale shark interaction happens at the surface in a few metres of water, so a light snorkel set matters more here than scuba kit.

siteSpecificGear.name: 3mm wetsuit
siteSpecificGear.reason: Whale shark season (Nov-Jan) brings the year's coolest water, around 25-28C.

notes: Whale shark season is roughly November-January, peaking in December. A strict interaction code applies: no touching, no flash photography, no diving down onto the sharks, and limits on swimmers in the water. The sharks are juveniles and the bay is shallow; Ghoubbet al-Kharab next door has strong tidal currents. Summer (Jun-Aug) is brutally hot on land and diving is unremarkable outside the plankton season.

name: Apo Island
description: A volcanic island off the southeastern tip of Negros, ringed by one of the world's first community-run marine reserves — local fishers closed 450 m of reef in 1982 and the recovery is now a textbook case. Green and hawksbill turtles graze the shallow hard-coral gardens in such numbers that divers routinely share a single bommie with several at once, often within 5 m of the surface. The sheltered western sanctuary (Chapel, Rock Point West) suits any diver, while Coconut Point on the exposed tip rips with current that pulls in schooling bigeye trevally and jacks. Banded sea kraits hunt the coral heads, and the slopes pack more than 650 fish species and some 400 corals. Visibility regularly opens past 30 m.
getThere: Fly into Dumaguete-Sibulan Airport (DGT) on Negros, served by daily flights from Manila and Cebu. From Dumaguete it is about a 45-minute drive south to the launch points at Malatapay (Zamboanguita) or the dive resorts along Dauin, then a 30-45 minute banca (outrigger) crossing to the island. Most divers base in Dauin or Dumaguete and visit Apo on day trips.
notes: Apo Island is a protected community marine sanctuary: a per-diver sanctuary/entrance fee applies and the no-take core has restricted entry points, so dive only with a registered local operator or guide. Coconut Point and the eastern side can have strong, unpredictable currents and downcurrents (advanced), while the western Chapel/Rock Point sanctuary is calm and shallow. Do not touch or chase the turtles. The amihan (northeast monsoon, roughly December-February) can make the exposed side rough.

name: HMS Maori
description: A Tribal-class Royal Navy destroyer that joined the 1941 hunt for the battleship Bismarck, HMS Maori was sunk by a German air raid in Valletta's Grand Harbour in February 1942. The salvaged forward section was later scuttled off Fort St Elmo, where it now lies broken and upright on sand at 12-16 m, making it Malta's shallowest wreck and an easy shore dive after a 120 m surface swim. Flattened hull plates and exposed framework shelter moray eels, octopus, scorpionfish and shoals of salema and bream, while nudibranchs, cuttlefish and the occasional seahorse work the wreckage for macro divers.
getThere: Fly into Malta International (MLA), about 25 minutes by car or bus from Valletta. The wreck is a shore dive in St Elmo Bay below Fort St Elmo, near the entrance to Marsamxett Harbour on the city's seaward side. Gear up at the small parking area, descend the concrete steps and platform into the water, then swim roughly 120 m out to the wreck.
notes: Shore entry is over concrete steps, a platform and rocks that get very slippery; steel railings aid the exit. The wreck sits near a busy harbour mouth, so carry a surface marker buoy and watch for boat traffic. Reports of vehicle break-ins at the parking area mean you should leave nothing visible in your car.

name: Columbia Reef
description: A drift along the southern Cozumel reef where coral buttresses build into tall pinnacles riddled with swim-throughs, arches, and sand chutes, draped in barrel and vase sponges and sea plumes. The deep section starts near 18 m and drops past 40 m into blue water, with a gentler coral plateau running above it. Hawksbill and green turtles graze the coral, spotted eagle rays and nurse sharks patrol the drop-off, and the Cozumel-endemic splendid toadfish tucks into reef crevices. A light-to-moderate current carries divers north along the structure.
getThere: Fly to Cozumel International Airport (CZM). Dive boats leave from San Miguel and the southern marinas; the southern reefs are roughly a 30-45 minute boat ride. Columbia is a boat-only live-drift dive.
notes: Inside Parque Nacional Arrecifes de Cozumel; a marine park fee/wristband is required and anchoring is banned. Dived as a live-boat drift, so deploy a surface marker buoy on ascent. The deep pinnacles and swim-throughs make it an advanced dive, while the adjacent Columbia Shallows is far easier.

name: Snake Gully
description: A cavern carved beneath Niue's limestone reef flat, reached by boat about 30 metres off Avatele Beach on the southwest coast. Divers drop from the reef top near 8 metres through swim-throughs to around 20, where ledges and overhangs shelter katuali — the black-and-white banded sea krait found nowhere on Earth but Niue. The venomous yet placid snakes thread between divers alongside Napoleon wrasse, regal angelfish, whitetip reef sharks, barracuda, moray eels and turtles. With no rivers and porous limestone shedding almost no sediment, visibility routinely runs 30-60 metres and can reach 80. From July to September migrating humpback whales calve offshore and their song carries into the cavern.
getThere: Niue International Airport (IUE) at Alofi is served by Air New Zealand from Auckland (about 3.5 hours), with one to two flights a week. Dive operators based in Alofi run boats to the west-coast sites; Snake Gully lies roughly 30 metres off Avatele Beach on the southwest coast, a short ride from Alofi wharf, where boats are craned in and out because the island has no sheltered harbour.
notes: Katuali are highly venomous but non-aggressive — observe, never handle them. Niue has no recompression chamber, so dive conservatively; the nearest is in New Zealand. Boats launch by crane at Alofi wharf and trips depend on west-coast swell. Scuba is seasonal — the local operator typically closes from around December to February.

name: Cabo Pearce
description: A long finger of solidified lava that juts off the east coast of Socorro Island and runs out into open water, its top shelf around 15-20m before the ridge plunges past 30m into the blue. Currents sweeping along the ridge feed a cleaning station that draws Socorro's exceptionally interactive giant oceanic mantas (Mobula birostris) - wingspans to 7m - which hover over divers' exhaust bubbles at arm's length. A resident pod of bottlenose dolphins routinely approaches divers, while schooling scalloped hammerheads, silky and Galapagos sharks patrol the deeper edge. The usual tactic is to wedge into the rocks out of the current and let the pelagic traffic come to you.

conditionsByMonth.suitRecommendation: 3-5mm full (late season; most liveaboards wind down)

getThere: Fly into Los Cabos International Airport (SJD) in Baja California Sur. Liveaboards depart Cabo San Lucas or San Jose del Cabo; the open-ocean crossing takes 24-30 hours, and Socorro Island is the southernmost stop, usually an additional run beyond San Benedicto. There is no day-boat or shore access - the Revillagigedo Archipelago is reachable only by permitted liveaboard.
notes: Inside Revillagigedo National Park / UNESCO World Heritage Site (2016), Mexico's largest fully protected marine reserve; access requires permits held by licensed liveaboards. Touching mantas and dolphins is prohibited. Strong along-ridge currents and occasional down-currents along the wall; the bottom drops well past 40m, so a reverse profile and good gas management matter. Most operators require 50+ logged dives and advanced/nitrox certification. Best plan is a negative entry onto the ridge, then shelter among the rocks and wait for mantas and dolphins to approach.

name: Abu Dabbab
description: A sheltered horseshoe bay 34 km north of Marsa Alam where a broad seagrass meadow fills the sandy centre between two fringing coral reefs. The grass is grazing ground for the bay's resident dugong — the 'sea cow' that gives the site its fame — and for giant green and hawksbill turtles that crop the blades at arm's length. Halavi guitarfish lie half-buried on the sand, seahorses and pipefish shelter in the seagrass, and the north and south reefs hold the usual Red Sea anthias, butterflyfish and morays. Most of the action sits between 5 and 12 m, with the outer reefs sloping past 18 m. Calm, near current-free and entered straight off the beach, it suits snorkellers, first dives and macro shooters alike — though dugong sightings are never guaranteed.
getThere: Fly into Marsa Alam International (RMF), about 35 km south of the bay (roughly 40 minutes by road). Abu Dabbab sits off the main coastal highway between the Malikia and Hilton Nubian resorts, signed down a short access road to the public beach. Most divers come on a shore-dive day trip from Marsa Alam, Port Ghalib or El Quseir dive centres and wade in straight off the sand. Hurghada (HRG) is a 3-4 hour drive north.

siteSpecificGear.name: Surface marker buoy
siteSpecificGear.reason: Boats and watersports cross the open bay; mark your position before surfacing on the outer reefs

siteSpecificGear.name: Macro lens or close-up filter
siteSpecificGear.reason: Seahorses, pipefish and nudibranchs hide in the seagrass and along the reef flanks

notes: Shore dive with an easy wade-in entry; the bay is a protected area and a small beach access fee usually applies. Dugong and turtles are wild and free-ranging — keep your distance, never chase, touch or block their route to the surface, and avoid finning up the seagrass. Boat traffic crosses the bay, so stay within the marked swim zones in the shallows and deploy an SMB on the outer reefs. Dugong sightings are unpredictable and can never be guaranteed.

name: Crystal Bay (Nusa Penida)
description: A sheltered bay on Nusa Penida's northwest coast where a sand-floored channel slides past coral slopes to 30–40 m and a rocky islet at the entrance hides a shallow swim-through cavern. From July to October, cold upwelling off the Lombok Strait pulls Mola alexandrini — the bumphead ocean sunfish, often topping 2 m — up from deep water to reef cleaning stations around 20–40 m, where bannerfish pick parasites from their flanks. The same chilled water can fire abrupt down-currents along the outer channel. Glassy visibility, frequently past 25 m, gives the site its name.
getThere: Fly into Ngurah Rai International (DPS) on Bali. Fast boats run from Sanur to Toyapakeh or Banjar Nyuh on Nusa Penida (~45 minutes), from where Crystal Bay is a short drive to the northwest coast and a beach or jukung entry. Most operators also day-trip directly by speedboat from Sanur, Padangbai, or Candidasa, reaching the bay in roughly 60–90 minutes.

siteSpecificGear.name: 5mm wetsuit + hood
