siteSpecificGear.reason: Lombok Strait upwelling drops water to 18–20 °C through a sharp thermocline in the July–October mola season — far colder than the 3mm most divers bring from Tulamben or Sanur.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB and solid buoyancy skills
siteSpecificGear.reason: Crystal Bay is known for sudden down-currents along the outer channel toward the pinnacle; divers must be able to swim off the reef and signal a drifting boat.

notes: Advanced certification and good buoyancy recommended: down-currents near the channel and depths of 20–40 m for mola sightings make this unsuitable for new divers despite the calm-looking bay. Peak mola season (Aug–Sep) draws crowds and boat traffic. Do not chase or crowd the sunfish — hold position and let them approach the cleaning station. Marine park / mooring fees apply.

name: Sha'ab Samadai (Dolphin House)
description: Crescent-shaped reef about 11 km southeast of Marsa Alam whose horseshoe arms enclose a shallow turquoise lagoon. A resident pod of 60-90 spinner dolphins (Stenella longirostris) returns to the lagoon each day to rest and nurse after feeding in open water overnight, making this one of the few reliable places on earth to share the water with wild dolphins. The site is a HEPCA-managed reserve split into three zones: an inner dolphin sanctuary closed to people, a snorkeling belt, and an outer diving zone of coral pinnacles, drop-offs and swim-throughs with a canyon on the western edge. Hard- and soft-coral gardens, reef fish, turtles and occasional reef sharks fill the outer slopes. Lagoon conditions are easy; the outer reef drops past 30 m.
getThere: Fly into Marsa Alam International (RMF), roughly 30 km north of the reef. Dolphin House is a 45-90 minute day-boat ride from Marsa Alam, Port Ghalib or Hamata marinas; southern Red Sea liveaboards also call here. Access requires a HEPCA permit, which dive and snorkel operators arrange as part of the trip.

siteSpecificGear.name: Snorkel set
siteSpecificGear.reason: The dolphin encounter happens in the shallow lagoon and snorkeling belt, not on scuba

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB
siteSpecificGear.reason: Boat traffic is heavy inside the managed area and drift exits run along the outer reef

notes: HEPCA manages Samadai as a protected dolphin reserve with a daily cap of about 10 boats, 175 snorkelers and 100 divers; operators must hold a permit. Three zones are buoyed: Zone A (inner lagoon) is a dolphin sanctuary closed to all people, Zone B is for guided snorkeling, and scuba diving is confined to Zone C on the outer reef. Snorkel boats are admitted roughly 10:00-14:00 and dive boats 09:00-15:00. Do not chase, touch or feed the dolphins; let them approach. Day-boat or liveaboard only, no shore access.

name: The Landslide (El Derrumbe)
description: A boulder slope on Wolf Island's southeast corner that looks like the cliff let go yesterday — broken rock and rubble spilling into the blue. Drop to 10 m and work down to a cleaning station where scalloped hammerheads stack up by the dozen while barberfish pick them clean. Galápagos sharks patrol the edge; from June to November whale sharks, many of them pregnant females, cruise the deep water beyond. Moray eels jam the cracks, and eagle rays and yellowfin tuna ride past in the current.
getThere: No international flights serve the Galápagos — fly into Quito (UIO) or Guayaquil (GYE) on mainland Ecuador, then a ~2-hour LATAM or Avianca flight to Baltra (GPS) or San Cristóbal (SCY). Wolf Island sits ~160 km north of the central islands and is reachable only by liveaboard; expect a 14–16 hour overnight crossing from the central archipelago.

siteSpecificGear.name: Coldwater wetsuit + hood
siteSpecificGear.reason: Humboldt upwellings drop temp to 19°C in season — third dive of the day is the cold one.

siteSpecificGear.name: Reef hook
siteSpecificGear.reason: Hooking is the only way to hold the line while hammerheads pass.

siteSpecificGear.name: Nautilus Lifeline or PLB
siteSpecificGear.reason: Surface separations have happened here. PLB is standard kit.

notes: Galápagos Marine Reserve park fees and a licensed dive guide are mandatory, and Wolf is liveaboard-only. Strong, shifting currents and occasional surface separations — a reef hook and a PLB or Nautilus Lifeline are standard kit. Advanced certification with recent cold-water and current experience expected.

name: Hin Daeng / Hin Muang
description: Two open-Andaman pinnacles about 500 m apart, 70 km southwest of Koh Lanta. Hin Daeng ('Red Rock') rises from roughly 50 m and is sheeted in red soft coral; Hin Muang ('Purple Rock') is Thailand's tallest submerged wall, dropping past 70 m and carpeted in purple soft coral. Oceanic mantas up to 4 m across circle the pinnacles to feed on the current, and whale sharks pass through from February to April. The exposed location draws strong current and has no surface marker, so every dive is a drift that ends mid-water under an SMB.
getThere: Krabi (KBV) and Phuket (HKT) are the nearest international airports. Koh Lanta is the standard launch point, with day-trip speedboats running roughly 2.5-3.5 hours each way; some Phuket, Phi Phi, and Koh Lipe operators also reach the site on longer day trips or liveaboards.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB
siteSpecificGear.reason: Open-ocean pinnacles with no permanent mooring; every dive surfaces mid-water on a drift.

siteSpecificGear.name: Reef hook
siteSpecificGear.reason: Hook in on the up-current face to hold position while mantas pass overhead.

notes: Inside Mu Ko Lanta National Park, usually closed mid-May to mid-October during the southwest monsoon. Exposed, deep, strong-current site - advanced certification and Nitrox recommended; surface conditions can deteriorate quickly.

name: Protea Banks
description: A deep offshore reef 7.5 km off Shelly Beach on the KwaZulu-Natal south coast, running roughly 6 km between a Northern and Southern Pinnacle at 27-40 m. Agulhas-fed current draws seven shark species: bull (Zambezi) sharks year-round, schooling scalloped hammerheads in spring and early summer, ragged-tooth sharks gathering to mate in winter, and tiger sharks in autumn. Sardine Run baitfish funnel through in June and July. Dived as a current drift, often on a baited line, with the reef deep enough that bottom time is short.
getThere: Fly into King Shaka International Airport (Durban), then drive about 2 hours south to Shelly Beach/Margate on the KwaZulu-Natal south coast. Dives are surf-launched by RIB from Shelly Beach harbour, reaching the reef in 20-30 minutes.
notes: Deep, current-swept advanced reef reached only by boat; surf launches are weather-dependent and trips cancel in big swell. Most dives are baited shark drifts - good buoyancy and nitrox recommended, and short no-deco bottom times at 30m+. Nearest recompression chamber is in Durban.
